rawCorpus.summary: The terminal PTY bridge on the macOS host is the SSH-like text path of the hybrid architecture: the host spawns a login shell inside a POSIX pseudo-terminal, streams the raw byte stream (VT escape sequences) to the client, and routes keystrokes back to PTY stdin. This completely sidesteps macOS CGEvent injection limitations because input is written directly to the PTY master file descriptor rather than being synthesized as keyboard events. The recommended design uses forkpty()/openpty() (from <util.h> on Darwin) for PTY allocation, DispatchIO for non-blocking reads on the master FD, TIOCSWINSZ + SIGWINCH for window resize, and a plain TCP stream transport over Network.framework — the simplest protocol that fits LAN use and avoids the complexity of mosh's state-machine UDP approach. App Sandbox is the hard constraint: if the host component is sandboxed it cannot spawn a shell directly; the practical solution is a non-sandboxed helper binary (launchd agent / XPC service without the sandbox entitlement) or shipping as a non-sandboxed app distributed outside the Mac App Store.

techniques.name: forkpty() — single-call PTY + fork + execve
techniques.how: forkpty(&master, NULL, NULL, &winsize) from <util.h> (Darwin) atomically allocates a PTY pair, calls fork(), calls login_tty() on the child side, and returns the master FD in the parent. In the child, execvp() launches the login shell. SwiftTerm/Pty.swift (PseudoTerminalHelpers.fork(andExec:)) uses exactly this pattern: build null-terminated C string arrays for args/env, call forkpty(), return (pid, masterFd) to the caller. The master FD becomes the single bidirectional channel for all terminal I/O.
techniques.impact: Zero-overhead text path — PTY bytes go master FD → transport → client. No pixel capture, no video encode. Latency is dominated by network RTT, not encode/decode. Eliminates need for CGEvent injection entirely for the terminal path.

techniques.name: openpty() + posix_spawn — safer alternative for Swift
techniques.how: openpty(&master, &slave, NULL, &termp, &winp) allocates the PTY pair without forking. The child is then launched via posix_spawn (or Foundation.Process) with the slave FD redirected to stdin/stdout/stderr using a posix_spawnattr file-actions structure. POSIX_SPAWN_SETSID creates a new session. login_tty(slave) is called from a pre-spawn configurator to set the controlling terminal. SwiftTerm's Subprocess path (guarded by #if false canImport(Subprocess)) demonstrates this via PlatformOptions.preSpawnProcessConfigurator setting POSIX_SPAWN_SETSID. The master FD is kept in the parent for I/O. Note: forkpty() itself is considered unsafe to call from Swift directly (the fork-then-Swift-runtime interaction), which is why SwiftTerm wraps it in a C helper (PseudoTerminalHelpers, compiled as C via the Darwin module import).
techniques.impact: Slightly more complex but avoids the fork-then-Swift-runtime hazard. Same I/O characteristics as forkpty once running. Required when using Swift Subprocess package.

techniques.name: DispatchIO stream on master FD for async reads
techniques.how: After forkpty(), wrap childfd in a DispatchIO(type: .stream, fileDescriptor: childfd). Call io.setLimit(lowWater: 1) and io.setLimit(highWater: 128*1024). Chain reads with io.read(offset:0, length:readSize, queue:readQueue) in the completion handler. Writes go via DispatchIO.write(toFileDescriptor:childfd, data:, ...). SwiftTerm uses a pendingChunks queue with a 4ms flush timeslice to coalesce bursts before dispatching to the delegate. Monitor process exit with DispatchSource.makeProcessSource(identifier: shellPid, eventMask: .exit). Clean up: close the FD in the DispatchIO cleanupHandler, not in deinit, to avoid EV_VANISHED crashes.
techniques.impact: Non-blocking, back-pressure-aware I/O on the PTY master. No poll() loop needed. 128 KB high-water mark absorbs large terminal output bursts (e.g., cat of a large file). Coalescing reduces dispatch overhead during fast output.

techniques.name: TIOCSWINSZ + SIGWINCH for window resize
techniques.how: When the client reports a new terminal size, call ioctl(masterFd, TIOCSWINSZ, &winsize) on the master FD. The kernel then sends SIGWINCH to the foreground process group of the PTY. The shell and any running programs (vim, tmux, etc.) respond by re-querying TIOCGWINSZ and reflowing their output. The winsize struct has four fields: ws_col (columns), ws_row (rows), ws_xpixel (pixel width, can be 0), ws_ypixel (pixel height, can be 0). SwiftTerm.PseudoTerminalHelpers.setWinSize() wraps exactly this: ioctl(masterPtyDescriptor, TIOCSWINSZ, &windowSize). On macOS the ioctl constant is typed as Int32 (unlike Linux where it needs UInt cast). Mosh-server demonstrates the full pattern: on receiving a Resize action from the client UserStream, it calls TIOCGWINSZ then TIOCSWINSZ on the host_fd (master PTY FD).
techniques.impact: Essential for correct terminal behavior. Without it, editors like vim, tmux splits, and line-wrapped output all break. Zero bandwidth cost — resize is a rare in-band control event.

techniques.name: Login shell setup: TERM, LANG, UTF-8 locale, login_tty
techniques.how: Set these env vars before execvp in the child: TERM=xterm-256color (or xterm), LANG=en_US.UTF-8 (critical — without it vi/ncurses emit ISO 2022 sequences), COLORTERM=truecolor for true-color terminals, mirror LOGNAME/USER/HOME/DISPLAY from the parent environment. Do NOT forward PATH blindly from the server process's environment. Mosh-server additionally sets NCURSES_NO_UTF8_ACS=1 (forces ncurses to use UTF-8 box-drawing instead of VT100 line-drawing), IUTF8 termios flag (tcgetattr/tcsetattr with c_iflag |= IUTF8) for correct backspace-over-multibyte-chars, and unsets STY (prevents GNU screen from thinking it's nested). Prepend '-' to argv[0] of the shell name to make it a login shell (e.g., '-zsh'), causing it to source .zprofile/.zshrc. SwiftTerm.Terminal.getEnvironmentVariables() provides the Swift reference implementation.
techniques.impact: Determines whether the user's shell starts correctly, whether tools like vim/tmux render box-drawing characters, and whether UTF-8 input/output works. Skipping IUTF8 causes backspace to corrupt multibyte characters.

techniques.name: TCP stream transport over Network.framework (plain byte relay)
techniques.how: The simplest wire protocol for LAN use: open an NWConnection (Network.framework) with .tcp parameters on a chosen port. Prepend each PTY data chunk with a 4-byte big-endian length header (the ET BackedWriter pattern: messageSize = htonl(packet.length()); prepend '0000' then overwrite with messageSize). Read the 4-byte header first, then read the declared number of body bytes. Optionally use NWProtocolFramer for framing at the framework level. Resize events are a separate message type (e.g., a 1-byte type prefix '1' + JSON/binary {cols, rows}). For the terminal path no further framing is needed — raw PTY bytes are forwarded verbatim, the client's SwiftTerm terminal emulator handles VT interpretation. TLS via NWProtocolTLS can be layered on top if desired. On LAN with typical RTT of < 1ms, TCP head-of-line blocking is irrelevant.
techniques.impact: Minimal code, zero external dependencies, full Apple-native. NWConnection handles reconnection via .waitingForConnectivity path. Idle efficiency is excellent — the PTY master FD produces no data when the shell is idle, so no bytes flow. No encode/decode overhead.

techniques.name: ET-style TCP reconnection (BackedWriter/BackedReader)
techniques.how: Eternal Terminal implements EternalTCP: BackedWriter maintains an encrypted circular buffer of the last N sent packets indexed by a monotonically increasing sequence number. BackedReader tracks how many packets it has consumed. On reconnect, both sides exchange a SequenceHeader protobuf (just the sequence number), then BackedWriter sends a CatchupBuffer of the missing packets (writer.sequenceNumber - reader.sequenceNumber messages). The receiver calls revive(newSocketFd, newLocalEntries) which inserts the recovered messages and advances its sequence number. Wire format: 4-byte big-endian length header + serialized encrypted packet. This is TCP-over-TCP reconnect, not UDP — appropriate for LAN where loss is rare and in-order delivery matters for terminal state.
techniques.impact: Seamless session survival across LAN disruptions (Wi-Fi handoff, sleep/wake). Terminal state is preserved without user seeing garbled output. Critical for coding sessions over Wi-Fi.

techniques.name: SSH RFC 4254 channel protocol — reference design for framing
techniques.how: SSH uses multiplexed channels over one encrypted TCP stream. Relevant messages: SSH_MSG_CHANNEL_DATA (1-byte type, 4-byte channel ID, 4-byte+data string) carries raw PTY output/input. SSH_MSG_CHANNEL_REQUEST with 'pty-req' allocates a PTY (sends TERM string, cols, rows, pixel width/height, encoded terminal modes). SSH_MSG_CHANNEL_REQUEST with 'window-change' (no reply expected) sends col/row/pixel resize. Flow control uses SSH_MSG_CHANNEL_WINDOW_ADJUST (4-byte bytes-to-add). For a LAN-only app these multiplexing/flow-control mechanics are unnecessary overhead — a direct TCP connection with a trivial framing protocol is simpler. The PTY-req and window-change message structures are useful as reference for encoding terminal mode parameters (opcode-uint32 pairs terminated by TTY_OP_END=0x00).
techniques.impact: Reference only for this architecture. The SSH channel model is designed for WAN with multiplexing and crypto overhead. For a direct LAN connection, adopting only the window-change payload encoding (cols/rows as uint32s) is useful.

techniques.name: Mosh SSP (State Synchronization Protocol) over UDP — reference for understanding
techniques.how: SSP synchronizes a Terminal.Complete object (full screen state) from server to client using UDP datagrams. Wire format: each datagram = 8-byte nonce (big-endian uint64) + AES-128-OCB ciphertext (plaintext + 16-byte authentication tag). The plaintext is a protobuf Instruction with fields: protocol_version, old_num (state the diff is based on), new_num (state being sent), ack_num (last remote state received), throwaway_num (states safe to discard), diff (serialized state delta), chaff (up to 16 random bytes for traffic analysis resistance). Fragmentation: if the diff exceeds MTU - 28 bytes (Connection::ADDED_BYTES=12 + Crypto::Session::ADDED_BYTES=16), it is split into fragments identified by (id, fragment_num). MTU constants: DEFAULT_IPV4_MTU=1280, DEFAULT_IPV6_MTU=1280, DEFAULT_SEND_MTU=500 (fallback). Timing: SEND_INTERVAL_MIN=20ms, SEND_INTERVAL_MAX=250ms, send_interval = ceil(SRTT/2) clamped to [20,250]ms. Heartbeat: ACK_INTERVAL=3000ms empty acks when idle. SEND_MINDELAY=8ms minimum between consecutive sends. The server runs a full terminal emulator (Terminal::Complete) and only sends the diff from what the client is assumed to have.
techniques.impact: Mosh's approach is optimal for lossy WAN (skips intermediate frames, always delivers latest state). For LAN with <1ms RTT and <0.01% loss, the complexity is unjustified — raw byte streaming over TCP performs equally well with far less implementation work. Do not adopt SSP for LAN.

techniques.name: ttyd WebSocket framing — reference for type-prefixed messages
techniques.how: ttyd uses libwebsockets over TCP/HTTP. Message framing: each WebSocket binary message has a 1-byte type prefix. Server-to-client: '0' (OUTPUT) = PTY bytes, '1' (SET_WINDOW_TITLE), '2' (SET_PREFERENCES). Client-to-server: '0' (INPUT) = keystrokes to pty_write(), '1' (RESIZE_TERMINAL) = JSON {cols,rows} to pty_resize(), '2'/'3' (PAUSE/RESUME), '{' (JSON_DATA for initial auth/setup). This 1-byte-prefix pattern is clean and easy to implement over any transport.
techniques.impact: The 1-byte type prefix framing pattern (OUTPUT='0', INPUT='0', RESIZE='1') is a useful template for the custom LAN protocol. Adopted directly: one byte message type + raw payload. No JSON overhead for the hot path (terminal bytes).

techniques.name: Scrollback: PTY produces no scrollback — handle on client or via tmux
techniques.how: A raw PTY has no built-in scrollback buffer. All output flows from the master FD as a byte stream; once read, it is gone from the OS buffer. Options: (1) Client-side scrollback: SwiftTerm maintains a scrollback buffer in its Terminal object (in-memory ring of terminal lines). This is the default for local processes. For remote use, the server sends all PTY bytes over the wire and the client Terminal accumulates them. (2) tmux on the server: running tmux inside the PTY gives server-side scrollback with tmux control mode (-CC) for structured access, but adds complexity. (3) Keep a server-side ring buffer of the last N bytes (e.g., 1MB) to support reconnect replay, similar to ET's BackedWriter but for the terminal stream. For the hybrid app the client-side approach (SwiftTerm handles scrollback) is simplest and keeps the server stateless.
techniques.impact: Client-side scrollback via SwiftTerm is zero-cost on the server. The wire carries the raw byte stream; the client's terminal emulator maintains history. If reconnection replay is needed, a server-side ring buffer of raw PTY bytes (not parsed terminal state) is simpler than full mosh-style state sync.

techniques.name: App Sandbox: shell-spawning requires non-sandboxed component
techniques.how: A sandboxed macOS app cannot call forkpty()/execvp() to spawn an arbitrary login shell — the sandbox blocks exec of external processes not declared in the entitlements, and there is no entitlement to whitelist arbitrary shell execution. The Apple-approved patterns are: (1) A non-sandboxed LaunchDaemon or LaunchAgent helper binary (installed to /Library/LaunchDaemons/ or ~/Library/LaunchAgents/), communicating with the sandboxed app via XPC or a local TCP socket. (2) Distribute the host component as a non-sandboxed app via Developer ID (outside the Mac App Store) — most developer tools (Xcode, VS Code, iTerm2, Terminal.app itself) are not sandboxed. (3) A privileged helper installed via SMJobBless/SMAppService. For a LAN remote-control tool aimed at developers, option 2 (non-sandboxed Developer ID app) is the standard approach and removes all sandbox restrictions on forkpty, PTY, and socket binding.
techniques.impact: This is an architectural decision, not a runtime optimization. Shipping outside the Mac App Store with Developer ID removes all constraints on PTY spawning. Attempting to sandbox the host side adds significant complexity with no user-visible benefit for a developer tool.

open_questions: Should the custom TCP protocol use a length-prefix framing (4-byte big-endian, ET style) or a type-prefix framing (1-byte type + length, ttyd style)? The ttyd approach is cleaner for multiplexing resize events alongside data.

For reconnection replay: is a server-side raw-byte ring buffer (last 1MB of PTY output) sufficient, or does the client need to re-render from the ring buffer through its terminal emulator? Raw bytes are simpler but the client must replay VT escape sequences on reconnect.
Does the hybrid app need multiplexing of multiple PTY sessions over one TCP connection (like SSH channels), or is one TCP connection per terminal session acceptable on LAN?
Should the host PTY bridge run as a persistent launchd agent (always-on, low-latency first connection) or be launched on-demand via XPC when the client connects?

How should the architecture handle the case where the user closes the lid (macOS sleep) while a remote PTY session is active — does the TCP connection survive via Network.framework's .waitingForConnectivity or does ET-style reconnection need to be implemented?

What TERM value should be advertised when the client is an iOS device — xterm-256color is correct for SwiftTerm, but if the client terminal emulator is different (e.g., an iOS app using a custom renderer) the TERM value may need to be configurable.

verifications.claim: forkpty() is unsafe to call directly from Swift (assertion from Apple Developer Forums thread/688534 and SwiftTerm comments) — verify whether this applies to Swift 5.9+ with the new concurrency model or only to Swift+Objective-C mixed runtimes

verdict.claim: forkpty() is unsafe to call directly from Swift (assertion from Apple Developer Forums thread/688534 and SwiftTerm comments) — and the question is whether this applies to Swift 5.9+ with the new concurrency model or only to Swift+Objective-C mixed runtimes
verdict.evidence:
**The Apple Developer Forums thread 688534 was not directly readable** (HTTP 403 from developer.apple.com). No exact quote from it could be verified. The Swift Forums mirror thread (forums.swift.org/t/swift-process-with-psuedo-terminal/51457) contains only Quinn "The Eskimo!"'s remark that "pseudo terminals are a royal pain in… any language", with no specific claim that forkpty() is unsafe to call from Swift.

**SwiftTerm source code directly contradicts the claim.** The production implementation in [Sources/SwiftTerm/Pty.swift](https://github.com/migueldeicaza/SwiftTerm/blob/main/Sources/SwiftTerm/Pty.swift) calls `forkpty()` directly from Swift, with zero safety warnings, caveats, or workaround comments in the file. The `startProcessWithForkpty` path in [Sources/SwiftTerm/LocalProcess.swift](https://github.com/migueldeicaza/SwiftTerm/blob/main/Sources/SwiftTerm/LocalProcess.swift) is the non-disabled default path (the `Subprocess`-based alternative is wrapped in `#if false`). SwiftTerm is actively maintained and used in production macOS/iOS terminal emulators with no recorded issues around this call.

**The actual safety boundary is the child side, not the call site.** The well-documented macOS fork-safety constraint (Apple ObjC runtime, introduced in macOS 10.13 High Sierra) fires when Objective-C `+initialize` methods are invoked *between* fork() and exec*(). The forkpty() pattern specifically: (1) calls forkpty() in the parent — safe, no ObjC between fork and exec; (2) in the child, immediately calls `execve()` (or `_exit(127)` on failure) — this is exactly the "fork-then-exec-immediately" pattern that Apple's own documentation says is always safe. The ObjC runtime crash only happens when code *runs in the forked child* before exec replaces the image.

**The "Swift concurrency model" framing is a mismatch for this problem.** The fork-safety issue predates Swift structured concurrency and is about the ObjC runtime's `+initialize` mutex, not Swift's actor system or task system. Swift 5.9's concurrency model (actors, async/await, strict concurrency checking) does not interact with fork/forkpty in any documented way, and SwiftTerm's forkpty() call is synchronous, called from a regular method, not from an async context or actor.

**What the real constraint IS:** The parent process must not have other threads running Swift or ObjC code that are *mid-initialization* when forkpty() is called, and the child must not run any Swift/ObjC/libdispatch code after fork — it must exec immediately. SwiftTerm's Pty.swift does exactly this: pre-allocates all C strings with strdup() before the fork call, calls forkpty(), then in the child calls chdir() + execve() + _exit() — all pure POSIX, no Swift runtime involvement after fork. This is textbook-correct usage.

**Alternative path exists in SwiftTerm for future migration.** There is a commented-out `#if false //canImport(Subprocess)` block in LocalProcess.swift that uses `openpty()` + Swift's `Subprocess` framework (posix_spawn internally) to avoid forkpty entirely. This is present as a forward-looking alternative, not as a workaround for a current safety bug.
verdict.correction:
The claim, as stated, conflates two separate issues and overstates what the sources actually say.

**What is true:** It is unsafe to execute Swift/ObjC/libdispatch code *in the forked child process* after forkpty() before exec(). This is a real constraint that has been documented since macOS 10.13. It has nothing to do with Swift 5.9 concurrency or mixed ObjC runtimes specifically — it is a POSIX + ObjC-runtime property.

**What is false or unverifiable:** The claim that "forkpty() is unsafe to call directly from Swift" as a blanket statement. The call itself (in the parent process) is not unsafe. SwiftTerm calls forkpty() directly from Swift in production with no documented crashes or workarounds around this specific issue, and the child process implementation is correct (pure POSIX-only calls, immediate execve).

**Correct characterization:** forkpty() is safe to call from Swift provided:
1. All arguments (C strings, winsize) are prepared before the call using only POSIX-safe means (strdup, malloc).
2. The child process path executes only async-signal-safe / POSIX syscalls (chdir, execve, _exit) — no Swift runtime, no Foundation, no libdispatch, no Objective-C.
3. The parent path returns to normal Swift/ObjC code immediately — which is what forkpty() guarantees (the child branches away).

SwiftTerm's Pty.swift satisfies all three conditions. The `#if false //canImport(Subprocess)` alternative using openpty+posix_spawn exists as a modernization path, not a safety fix.

**Regarding Swift 5.9+ concurrency model:** This is a non-issue for forkpty(). The ObjC runtime fork-safety problem predates Swift concurrency and is orthogonal to actors/async-await. There is no documented interaction between Swift structured concurrency and forkpty(). If forkpty() is called from within an actor or async context, the risk is the same as calling it from any multithreaded context — no worse, no better.

verifications.claim: POSIX_SPAWN_SETSID availability in Swift Subprocess PlatformOptions.preSpawnProcessConfigurator on macOS — the SwiftTerm code for this is guarded by #if false canImport(Subprocess), meaning it is not yet in production

verdict.claim: POSIX_SPAWN_SETSID availability in Swift Subprocess PlatformOptions.preSpawnProcessConfigurator on macOS — the SwiftTerm code for this is guarded by #if false canImport(Subprocess), meaning it is not yet in production
verdict.evidence:
Both halves of the claim are independently confirmed by primary source code:

HALF 1 — `POSIX_SPAWN_SETSID` via `preSpawnProcessConfigurator` is a real, production API in `swiftlang/swift-subprocess`.

`PlatformOptions.preSpawnProcessConfigurator` is declared `public` with no conditional compilation guard in [`Sources/Subprocess/Platforms/Subprocess+Darwin.swift` lines 81–87](https://sourcegraph.com/github.com/swiftlang/swift-subprocess@11633673a41f509f8945f23c96c7acd4adafd679/-/blob/Sources/Subprocess/Platforms/Subprocess+Darwin.swift?L81-87):

```swift
public var preSpawnProcessConfigurator:
    (
        @Sendable (
            inout posix_spawnattr_t?,
            inout posix_spawn_file_actions_t?
        ) throws -> Void
    )? = nil
```

The official README (lines 315–328) documents the exact POSIX_SPAWN_SETSID pattern as the canonical macOS escape hatch:

```swift
platformOptions.preSpawnProcessConfigurator = { spawnAttr, fileAttr in
    var flags: Int16 = 0
    posix_spawnattr_getflags(&spawnAttr, &flags)
    posix_spawnattr_setflags(&spawnAttr, flags | Int16(POSIX_SPAWN_SETSID))
}
```

[`Tests/SubprocessTests/DarwinTests.swift` lines 33–50](https://sourcegraph.com/github.com/swiftlang/swift-subprocess@11633673a41f509f8945f23c96c7acd4adafd679/-/blob/Tests/SubprocessTests/DarwinTests.swift?L33-50) has a live test (`testSubprocessPlatformOptionsProcessConfiguratorUpdateSpawnAttr`) that runs a subprocess with `POSIX_SPAWN_SETSID` and asserts a new session was created. The API is not experimental — it shipped in the public release and is exercised by the test suite.

HALF 2 — SwiftTerm's Subprocess integration is guarded `#if false` and therefore NOT active in production.

[`Sources/SwiftTerm/LocalProcess.swift` line 12](https://sourcegraph.com/github.com/migueldeicaza/SwiftTerm@9446f60557ed1bbea1b4720bd5884c2b3d3428be/-/blob/Sources/SwiftTerm/LocalProcess.swift?L12):

```swift
#if false //canImport(Subprocess)
import Subprocess
import System
#endif
```

The comment `//canImport(Subprocess)` is literally commented out; the outer condition evaluates to `false`, so the entire Subprocess import and every downstream block (`startProcessWithSubprocess`, `createPseudoTerminal`, `setupLoginTty`, the `Task {}` launch path) is dead code at compile time. All five `#if false //canImport(Subprocess)` guards in the file follow the same pattern (lines 12, 97, 204, 273, and within `startProcess` and `terminate`). The active production path is always `startProcessWithForkpty`.

ONE CORRECTION on repo identity: the claim says "apple/swift-subprocess". The canonical repository is `swiftlang/swift-subprocess` (https://github.com/swiftlang/swift-subprocess). There is no public `apple/swift-subprocess` repo. The package identity in Package.swift is `https://github.com/swiftlang/swift-subprocess.git`. This is a minor naming error in the claim but does not affect the substance.
verdict.correction: One naming error: the claim says "apple/swift-subprocess". The actual canonical repository is swiftlang/swift-subprocess (https://github.com/swiftlang/swift-subprocess). There is no public apple/swift-subprocess repo. Everything else in the claim is accurate.
