verdict.claim: IUTF8 termios flag availability on macOS Darwin — mosh guards this with #ifdef HAVE_IUTF8; verify that Darwin exposes IUTF8 in <termios.h> on current macOS versions
verdict.evidence: The Apple XNU kernel source (apple/darwin-xnu bsd/sys/termios.h line 133) explicitly defines IUTF8 = 0x00004000 with the comment "maintain state for UTF-8 VERASE", wrapped in the guard `#if !defined(_POSIX_C_SOURCE) || defined(_DARWIN_C_SOURCE)`. Under any standard (non-strict-POSIX) build, this symbol is visible. Mosh's configure.ac (line 528–532) uses AC_CHECK_DECL([IUTF8], ..., [[#include <termios.h>]]) — a compile-time probe that succeeds on Darwin under default Autoconf flags, so HAVE_IUTF8 is defined and the warning/fallback path is not triggered. mosh-server.cc (lines 555–569) then sets `child_termios.c_iflag |= IUTF8` unconditionally under #ifdef HAVE_IUTF8. Mosh lead developer Keith Winstein explicitly listed macOS alongside Linux as a platform where IUTF8 is implemented. No conflicting evidence was found.
verdict.correction: No correction needed. One nuance: the symbol is conditionally visible — it is hidden if the build explicitly sets -D_POSIX_C_SOURCE without -D_DARWIN_C_SOURCE. Under mosh's standard Autoconf probe (default flags), the symbol is detected and HAVE_IUTF8 is defined on Darwin. The flag affects canonical-mode VERASE (backspace deletes full UTF-8 character, not single byte); it has no effect in raw PTY mode.

rawCorpus.summary: Mosh's State Synchronization Protocol (SSP) and speculative local echo are the single biggest responsiveness levers for a hybrid terminal path. SSP transmits terminal *state diffs* (not byte streams) over UDP, making every datagram idempotent and dropped-packet-tolerant. The local echo / prediction engine runs a shadow terminal emulator on the client, speculatively renders keystrokes instantly, marks them with an underline until the server confirms them, and self-corrects silently on mismatch. In evaluation across 40 hours / 9,986 keystrokes: 70% of keystrokes received immediate confident display, only 0.9% required within-RTT correction. The entire prediction engine (PredictionEngine, ConditionalOverlayCell, ConditionalCursorMove) is portable logic with no OS dependency — it has already been ported to Go (nosshtradamus) wrapping the same C++ via CGo. A Swift port over Network.framework UDP is architecturally straightforward.

techniques.name: SSP: State-diff UDP transport (idempotent datagrams)
techniques.how: Instead of forwarding a raw byte stream (like SSH/TCP), mosh's Transport layer maintains a list of numbered terminal states. tick() calls diff_from(assumed_receiver_state) to compute the minimal protobuf diff, then sends it in a UDP datagram labelled with old_num/new_num. The receiver applies the diff to its own copy of old_num to reach new_num. Every datagram is idempotent: receiving the same datagram twice or out of order is safe — the receiver simply ignores datagrams whose old_num it no longer holds. There is no retransmission queue; instead, if no ACK arrives within timeout+ACK_DELAY, the sender falls back to diffing from the oldest known state (prophylactic resend). This means dropped packets never stall the stream — the next packet carries a self-sufficient diff.
techniques.impact: Eliminates TCP head-of-line blocking for the terminal path. Packet loss causes a slightly larger diff payload in the next packet, not a stall. On the LAN (sub-1ms RTT) this matters less, but it enables roaming and graceful degradation. For a coding tool this means Ctrl-C and terminal resets are never blocked behind a retransmission timeout.

techniques.name: SSP: Roaming — server tracks client IP from incoming packet source address
techniques.how: In Connection::recv_one(), the server unconditionally reads the source address from every received packet (via recvmsg() msg_name). If it differs from the current remote_addr, the server updates remote_addr and logs 'Server now attached to client at ...'. The client also periodically hops to a new UDP source port (hop_port()) if PORT_HOP_INTERVAL has elapsed without a successful round trip, to probe through NAT/firewall rebindings. No reconnect handshake is needed.
techniques.impact: For a LAN-only coding tool this is lower priority, but the same mechanism means the client can survive a brief Wi-Fi/Ethernet switch (common when docking a MacBook) without session loss.

techniques.name: SSP: AES-128-OCB authenticated encryption per datagram
techniques.how: Each UDP payload is encrypted with AES-128 in Offset Codebook (OCB) mode using a session key exchanged at startup via SSH. The 64-bit nonce encodes both direction (1 bit) and sequence number (63 bits), preventing replay across directions. The receiver checks p.direction and expected_receiver_seq; out-of-order packets below expected_receiver_seq are returned but not used for timestamp or targeting updates.
techniques.impact: Provides confidentiality and authentication without per-packet TCP overhead. On Apple, CryptoKit provides AES-GCM natively (OCB is not in CryptoKit — a custom implementation or use of CommonCrypto/libssl is needed for OCB specifically).

techniques.name: SSP: SRTT-based adaptive send rate (target: 2 frames per RTT)
techniques.how: send_interval() = ceil(SRTT / 2), clamped to [SEND_INTERVAL_MIN=20ms, SEND_INTERVAL_MAX=250ms]. SRTT is computed with standard RFC 6298 exponential smoothing (alpha=1/8, beta=1/4) from the round-trip timestamp embedded in each datagram header (two uint16 fields: timestamp sent, timestamp_reply echoing the received timestamp). A mindelay clock coalesces rapid keystrokes: the first pending change starts mindelay_clock, and next_send_time = max(mindelay_clock + SEND_MINDELAY, last_sent + send_interval). SEND_MINDELAY defaults to 8ms for the client (set to 1ms via set_send_delay(1) in stmclient.cc).
techniques.impact: On LAN (SRTT ~0.5ms) send_interval clamps to 20ms → max 50fps for server-to-client state updates. Keystroke input is sent with 1ms mindelay, meaning every keystroke goes to the server within ~1ms. This is the right operating point for a coding tool.

techniques.name: Speculative local echo — PredictionEngine: epoch-gated overlay cells
techniques.how: The PredictionEngine maintains two parallel data structures on top of the client's copy of the latest server framebuffer: (1) a list of ConditionalOverlayRow/ConditionalOverlayCell objects that replace specific (row, col) cells with predicted characters; (2) a list of ConditionalCursorMove objects that move the predicted cursor position. Each overlay cell/cursor carries: expiration_frame (the frame number after which confirmation is expected), tentative_until_epoch (don't display until this epoch is confirmed), and prediction_time (for glitch detection). When new_user_byte() receives a printable ASCII character (0x20–0x7e, width==1), it: (a) advances the predicted cursor one column, (b) stores the predicted character in the overlay cell for that (row, col), (c) tags both with the current prediction_epoch. On output, apply() layers the overlay on top of the server framebuffer before computing the display diff. Predictions are invisible until srtt_trigger or glitch_trigger fires.
techniques.impact: Zero-latency keystroke echo on the client — the character appears before the UDP packet even leaves the NIC. For a coding tool where the user types hundreds of keystrokes per minute, this is the dominant responsiveness win.

techniques.name: Speculative local echo — SRTT trigger and underline (flagging) with hysteresis
techniques.how: PredictionEngine::cull() checks send_interval (which tracks SRTT/2) against four thresholds defined in terminaloverlay.h: SRTT_TRIGGER_HIGH=30ms (>30ms activates predictions), SRTT_TRIGGER_LOW=20ms (<=20ms with no active predictions cures the trigger). FLAG_TRIGGER_HIGH=80ms (>80ms activates underlining/flagging), FLAG_TRIGGER_LOW=50ms (<=50ms cures flagging). Hysteresis prevents flicker. When srtt_trigger=true and flagging=false: predictions display without underline (appears native). When flagging=true: predictions display with the 'underlined' rendition attribute set on the cell. On LAN (SRTT ~0.5ms → send_interval=20ms), srtt_trigger stays false by default in Adaptive mode — predictions are NOT shown unless the glitch_trigger fires. This means on a fast LAN, predictions are dormant until a glitch occurs.
techniques.impact: On LAN: without a trigger, local echo in Adaptive mode does nothing — the round-trip is fast enough that the server echo arrives before the user notices. To get instant echo unconditionally on LAN, use DisplayPreference=Always. The hybrid design should default to Always for the terminal path since PTY round-trips via loopback/LAN are still 1-5ms.

techniques.name: Speculative local echo — glitch trigger for delayed confirmations
techniques.how: Even when SRTT is low, if a prediction remains unconfirmed for GLITCH_THRESHOLD=250ms, glitch_trigger is set to GLITCH_REPAIR_COUNT=10 (activates display only). If outstanding for GLITCH_FLAG_THRESHOLD=5000ms, glitch_trigger is set to GLITCH_REPAIR_COUNT*2=20 (activates display + underline). The glitch_trigger decrements by 1 for each prediction confirmed within GLITCH_THRESHOLD ms, with a minimum interval of GLITCH_REPAIR_MININTERVAL=150ms between decrements. This provides a 'burst mode' that activates predictions temporarily after a network hiccup without waiting for SRTT to climb.
techniques.impact: Handles intermittent congestion on an otherwise fast LAN without permanently activating underlining. Relevant for Wi-Fi networks with occasional retransmissions.

techniques.name: Speculative local echo — epoch system for safe self-correction on mismatch
techniques.how: When the server's confirmed framebuffer arrives and an overlay cell's server-confirmed content differs from the predicted content, the engine calls kill_epoch(cell.tentative_until_epoch). kill_epoch() removes all cursor predictions and overlay cells that are tentative in that epoch (tentative_until_epoch > epoch-1), then calls become_tentative() which increments prediction_epoch, marking all subsequent predictions as belonging to a new unconfirmed epoch. This means a single misprediction only kills the current epoch's unconfirmed predictions — older confirmed ones remain. Control characters (arrow keys, Escape, non-printable) also call become_tentative() because the engine cannot predict their effect. The client never shows stale/wrong text permanently: the next server frame corrects any discrepancy within one RTT.
techniques.impact: Makes local echo safe for interactive use — a shell using history completion or readline that produces unexpected output will self-correct within one round trip, with no user-visible corruption beyond a brief flicker.

techniques.name: Speculative local echo — backspace prediction with line-shift
techniques.how: In new_user_byte(), when the byte is 0x7f (DEL/backspace): cursor().col is decremented; then for each column from cursor().col to the right margin, the overlay cell is set to the content of the cell one position to the right (shifting the rest of the line left visually), except the rightmost cell which is marked unknown=true. If predict_overwrite mode is enabled, only the vacated cell is cleared. The 'unknown' flag causes apply() to set the underlined attribute on that cell without replacing its content, signalling uncertainty to the user.
techniques.impact: Backspace in a shell prompt feels instantaneous. The line-shift prediction correctly models how most shells redraw the line after backspace.

techniques.name: Speculative local echo — paste suppression
techniques.how: In STMClient::process_user_input(), if bytes_read > 100 (bulk paste), predictions are globally reset: overlays.get_prediction_engine().reset(). This prevents the engine from predicting a large paste block which would likely mismatch (shell may echo differently, readline may re-wrap lines) and would cause a disruptive correction flash.
techniques.impact: Avoids prediction-induced flicker on paste. Important for coding workflows where large code blocks are pasted.

techniques.name: nosshtradamus: mosh prediction engine ported to an SSH byte-stream interposer (Go + CGo)
techniques.how: nosshtradamus (thyth/nosshtradamus on GitHub) wraps the mosh C++ PredictionEngine via CGo in a go-mosh library. The Interposer struct implements io.ReadWriteCloser and sits between the SSH channel and the client terminal renderer. On Write() (user input), it calls predictor.NewUserByte() for each byte, increments an epoch counter, and calls predictor.LocalFrameSent(epoch). The openEpoch callback is called after the bytes are transmitted, and CloseEpoch() is called when the SSH server's response is received (out-of-band epoch tracking). This demonstrates that the prediction engine works on top of any latency-introducing transport (TCP/SSH), not just mosh's UDP SSP — the epoch mechanism is what matters, not the transport.
techniques.impact: Proof that the prediction engine is transport-agnostic. For the hybrid coding tool's terminal path (host PTY -> Network.framework UDP -> client), the same pattern applies: send keystroke bytes to PTY stdin, increment epoch, call LocalFrameSent; when the server's terminal state update arrives, call LocalFrameAcked + SetSendInterval(measured_RTT).

techniques.name: SwiftTerm integration point: feed() / TerminalDelegate.send()
techniques.how: SwiftTerm's Terminal class exposes two key APIs: (1) feed(byteArray: ArraySlice<UInt8>) — feeds server-side PTY output bytes into the terminal emulator, updating the internal framebuffer; this is called when UDP packets arrive from the host. (2) TerminalDelegate.send(source:data:) — called by the terminal when the user inputs (keyboard events generate escape sequences that the terminal emits back). For the hybrid tool: the client's Swift PredictionEngine shadow-runs the same framebuffer; on each keystroke, call prediction_engine.new_user_byte(byte, shadow_fb) before send() ships the byte to the host PTY via UDP. When the server's confirmed framebuffer arrives (from UDP state diff), call prediction_engine.cull(server_fb) + prediction_engine.apply(server_fb), then feed the result into SwiftTerm's display.
techniques.impact: SwiftTerm's architecture is designed to be renderer-agnostic. Inserting the PredictionEngine between the UDP receive path and the feed() call is a clean seam — no SwiftTerm modifications needed.

techniques.name: Network.framework NWConnection for UDP datagrams (Apple-native transport)
techniques.how: NWConnection with NWProtocolUDP.Options provides a non-blocking, send/receive per-datagram API. Create with NWEndpoint.hostPort, NWParameters.udp. send() takes a Content (Data), with a completion handler. receiveMessage() receives a single datagram with its metadata. The .batch { } API coalesces multiple send() calls into one kernel context switch — useful for sending both the keystroke payload datagram and a heartbeat ACK in the same batch. NWConnection handles multipath (MPTCP extension exists but UDP multipath is not standard) and interface changes through NWPathMonitor. For roaming, register an NWPathMonitor.pathUpdateHandler: when the path changes (e.g. Wi-Fi to Ethernet), call NWConnection.cancel() and create a new connection to the same host — or use NWConnection with .requiresFastOpenBeforeConnect disabled and rely on the server-side source-address tracking (as mosh does).
techniques.impact: Network.framework is the right transport layer on Apple platforms — it handles TLS, ECN, and has a clean Swift API. Replaces mosh's raw POSIX socket + recvmsg() + IP_RECVTOS with a higher-level API. The POSIX socket ECN handling in mosh (IP_RECVTOS, CONGESTION_TIMESTAMP_PENALTY) would need custom handling since NWConnection abstracts it.

key_settings: SEND_INTERVAL_MIN = 20ms (floor on inter-frame interval; caps server→client at 50fps)
SEND_INTERVAL_MAX = 250ms (ceiling; also the ACK heartbeat fallback rate)
ACK_INTERVAL = 3000ms (empty heartbeat ACK when no data to send)
ACK_DELAY = 100ms (delayed ACK coalescing window)
SEND_MINDELAY = 1ms (keystroke collection window on client side; set via set_send_delay(1))
SRTT_TRIGGER_HIGH = 30ms (> 30ms SRTT activates predictions in Adaptive mode)
SRTT_TRIGGER_LOW = 20ms (<= 20ms SRTT with no active predictions cures the trigger)
FLAG_TRIGGER_HIGH = 80ms (> 80ms SRTT activates underline/flagging)
FLAG_TRIGGER_LOW = 50ms (<= 50ms cures flagging)
GLITCH_THRESHOLD = 250ms (prediction outstanding this long = glitch, activates display)
GLITCH_FLAG_THRESHOLD = 5000ms (prediction outstanding 5s => activate underline)
GLITCH_REPAIR_COUNT = 10 (consecutive quick confirmations needed to cure glitch trigger)
GLITCH_REPAIR_MININTERVAL = 150ms (min interval between glitch_trigger decrements)
Paste suppression threshold = 100 bytes (bulk paste disables predictions for that batch)
Sent state queue limit = 32 entries (oldest 16 pruned when limit hit to bound memory)
Prophylactic resend: if diff-from-oldest < proposed_diff + 100 bytes AND < 1000 bytes, resend from oldest known state
ACTIVE_RETRY_TIMEOUT = 10000ms (retry unacknowledged state for up to 10s after last heard)
send_interval() formula: ceil(SRTT / 2), clamped to [SEND_INTERVAL_MIN, SEND_INTERVAL_MAX]
SRTT RFC 6298: alpha=1/8, beta=1/4; ignores RTT samples > 5000ms (Ctrl-Z protection)
DisplayPreference: Adaptive (default), Always, Never, Experimental (most aggressive — tentative=confirmed)
latency_numbers: Mosh over Sprint EV-DO 3G: median 5ms, mean 173ms keystroke-to-display latency (vs SSH median 503ms, mean 515ms)
70% of keystrokes received immediate display (confident prediction, no underline)
0.9% of predictions required within-RTT self-correction
40 hours, 9,986 keystrokes across 6 users evaluated in the USENIX ATC 2012 paper
send_interval on LAN (SRTT ~0.5ms): clamps to SEND_INTERVAL_MIN = 20ms → 50fps server-to-client
Keystroke send delay: 1ms mindelay → keystrokes reach server within ~1ms + RTT
SRTT trigger activates at 30ms (just above typical cross-continent ping)
Underlines activate at 80ms (transatlantic / intercontinental links)
Glitch display at 250ms outstanding, glitch underline at 5000ms outstanding
Server retransmission timeout floor: 50ms (vs TCP 1s minimum RTO)
Empty ACK heartbeat: every 3s when no data (keeps NAT alive, signals liveness)
Coalesce interval in nosshtradamus port: ~16ms (1/60s default, matching 60fps)
claims_to_verify: CLAIM: On LAN with Adaptive mode and SRTT < 20ms, mosh predictions are completely dormant (srtt_trigger=false) unless a glitch fires. Verify: terminaloverlay.cc cull() — srtt_trigger only flips true when send_interval > SRTT_TRIGGER_HIGH=30ms. On a sub-1ms LAN, send_interval clamps to 20ms, which is below 30ms. Therefore Adaptive mode gives NO local echo on LAN. The hybrid design must use DisplayPreference=Always for the terminal path.

CLAIM: AES-128-OCB is not available in Apple's CryptoKit (which only exposes AES-GCM and ChaChaPoly). A Swift implementation of SSP would need to use CommonCrypto, libssl, or replace OCB with AES-GCM (changing the wire format).

CLAIM: The mosh prediction engine is fully transport-agnostic (confirmed by nosshtradamus running it over TCP/SSH). Verify: terminaloverlay.cc has no dependency on mosh's UDP network layer — it only needs: local_frame_sent (last sent epoch), local_frame_acked (last ACKed epoch from server), local_frame_late_acked (echo_ack from latest remote state), send_interval (SRTT/2 estimate). All four can be maintained by any transport.

CLAIM: SwiftTerm's feed(byteArray:) and the Terminal internal framebuffer are sufficient to build a shadow state for the PredictionEngine. Verify: SwiftTerm Terminal.swift exposes buffer (internal), cols, rows — but the prediction engine needs direct cell access (get_cell(row, col)). SwiftTerm's public API is getCharData(col:row:) — verify this is sufficient for building a Framebuffer-equivalent that PredictionEngine.new_user_byte() can accept.

CLAIM: nosshtradamus uses the actual mosh C++ prediction engine via CGo (go-mosh). A pure Swift reimplementation would be needed for a native Swift app. The prediction logic in terminaloverlay.cc is ~750 lines of C++ — verify there is no existing Swift port before reimplementing.

CLAIM: Network.framework NWConnection does not expose per-packet ECN (Explicit Congestion Notification) bits to the application layer, unlike mosh's raw POSIX recvmsg() + IP_RECVTOS. The CONGESTION_TIMESTAMP_PENALTY feature of mosh would be unavailable. Verify in NWConnection documentation.
open_questions: For the LAN-only coding tool with PTY-over-UDP: should DisplayPreference always be Always (show predictions unconditionally without underline) rather than Adaptive, given that even LAN round trips are 1-5ms and the user expects instant echo?

The PTY terminal path sidesteps CGEvent injection — but confirming server state still requires a framebuffer diff. Does the server need to run a full terminal emulator (like mosh-server does with libvterm/mosh's own parser) to produce terminal state diffs, or is it acceptable to just stream raw PTY bytes and let the client run the emulator alone (as nosshtradamus does for SSH)?

Can the mosh PredictionEngine's ConditionalOverlayCell.apply() be bridged to SwiftTerm's Buffer/CharData model without a full framebuffer reimplementation in Swift? Or is a lightweight Swift port of the overlay logic more practical than CGo/C interop?
mosh sends random chaff (up to 16 bytes) per datagram to obscure keystroke timing via traffic analysis. Is this necessary for a LAN-only tool, or should it be omitted to reduce per-packet overhead?

The epoch ACK mechanism (local_frame_late_acked = echo_ack from server) requires the server to embed the last-seen client frame number in its outgoing packets (the echo_ack field in TransportInstruction protobuf). For a custom protocol, what is the minimal out-of-band signaling needed to implement this?
nosshtradamus reports its openEpoch callback fires after bytes are transmitted — how does the hybrid design handle this for UDP where there is no delivery confirmation below the SSP ack level?

verifications.claim: CLAIM: On LAN with Adaptive mode and SRTT < 20ms, mosh predictions are completely dormant (srtt_trigger=false) unless a glitch fires. Verify: terminaloverlay.cc cull() — srtt_trigger only flips true when send_interval > SRTT_TRIGGER_HIGH=30ms. On a sub-1ms LAN, send_interval clamps to 20ms, which is below 30ms. Therefore Adaptive mode gives NO local echo on LAN. The hybrid design must use DisplayPreference=Always for the terminal path.

verdict.claim: On LAN with Adaptive mode and SRTT < 20ms, mosh predictions are completely dormant (srtt_trigger=false) unless a glitch fires. In cull(), srtt_trigger only flips true when send_interval > SRTT_TRIGGER_HIGH=30ms. On a sub-1ms LAN, send_interval clamps to 20ms (SEND_INTERVAL_MIN), which is below 30ms. Therefore Adaptive mode gives NO local echo on LAN. The hybrid design must use DisplayPreference=Always for the terminal path.
verdict.correction: The claim is correct in its conclusion but slightly imprecise on one boundary: the srtt_trigger fires when send_interval > 30 (strict), and send_interval = ceil(SRTT/2) clamped to [20, 250]. The minimum floor of 20ms applies for all SRTT values below 40ms — not just sub-1ms. The trigger remains dormant until SRTT reaches ~61ms (send_interval = ceil(61/2) = 31 > 30). The claim's practical conclusion — "no local echo on LAN" — is correct for any typical LAN with SRTT under ~60ms.

verifications.claim: CLAIM: AES-128-OCB is not available in Apple's CryptoKit (which only exposes AES-GCM and ChaChaPoly). A Swift implementation of SSP would need to use CommonCrypto, libssl, or replace OCB with AES-GCM (changing the wire format).

verdict.claim: AES-128-OCB is not available in Apple's CryptoKit (which only exposes AES-GCM and ChaChaPoly). A Swift implementation of SSP would need to use CommonCrypto, libssl, or replace OCB with AES-GCM (changing the wire format).
verdict.evidence: CryptoKit exposes exactly two symmetric AEAD primitives — AES.GCM and ChaChaPoly — confirmed by Apple developer documentation and forum guidance. There is no AES.OCB type. Mosh's SSP uses AES-128-OCB3 (key length 16 bytes, confirmed in src/crypto/crypto.cc:159-160). The claim's list of alternatives is correct but the CommonCrypto path has an important nuance: CommonCrypto also does not expose OCB as a mode (no kCCModeOCB). Mosh's own macOS build (apple-common-crypto flag, configure.ac:418-424, described as 'internal OCB, Apple Common Crypto AES') uses CommonCrypto only as a raw AES-ECB block cipher primitive (kCCAlgorithmAES128 + kCCOptionECBMode, ocb_internal.cc:477-506), then applies a ~500-line pure-software OCB3 implementation on top. So 'use CommonCrypto' is correct only in the sense of using CommonCrypto for the underlying block cipher and porting mosh's software OCB layer, not because CommonCrypto exposes OCB natively. libssl (OpenSSL EVP_aes_128_ocb(), ocb_openssl.cc:45-46) is valid but requires separately installed OpenSSL on modern macOS, not the system stub.
verdict.correction: The claim is confirmed but the CommonCrypto alternative needs precision: CommonCrypto does not expose OCB as a mode. The correct path is to port mosh's ocb_internal.cc (its own software OCB3 implementation) using CommonCrypto only as a raw AES block cipher backend — exactly what mosh does on macOS today. The three viable options for a Swift SSP are: (1) port mosh's ocb_internal.cc with CommonCrypto as the AES primitive, (2) link a separately-installed OpenSSL and use EVP_aes_128_ocb(), or (3) replace OCB3 with AES-GCM and use CryptoKit.AES.GCM natively, accepting wire-format incompatibility with mosh.

verifications.claim: CLAIM: The mosh prediction engine is fully transport-agnostic (confirmed by nosshtradamus running it over TCP/SSH). Verify: terminaloverlay.cc has no dependency on mosh's UDP network layer — it only needs: local_frame_sent (last sent epoch), local_frame_acked (last ACKed epoch from server), local_frame_late_acked (echo_ack from latest remote state), send_interval (SRTT/2 estimate). All four can be maintained by any transport.

verdict.claim: The mosh prediction engine is fully transport-agnostic (confirmed by nosshtradamus running it over TCP/SSH). terminaloverlay.cc has no dependency on mosh's UDP network layer — it only needs: local_frame_sent (last sent epoch), local_frame_acked (last ACKed epoch from server), local_frame_late_acked (echo_ack from latest remote state), send_interval (SRTT/2 estimate). All four can be maintained by any transport.
verdict.evidence:
Source code analysis of the mosh HEAD clone (https://github.com/mobile-shell/mosh) confirms every sub-claim:

1. INTERFACE ISOLATION. terminaloverlay.h declares PredictionEngine with exactly four external setters: set_local_frame_sent(uint64_t), set_local_frame_acked(uint64_t), set_local_frame_late_acked(uint64_t), set_send_interval(unsigned int). terminaloverlay.cc includes src/frontend/terminaloverlay.h but has zero includes of any Network:: class. There is no transitive dependency on Connection, TransportSender, or any UDP socket primitive.

2. CALLER WIRING (stmclient.cc lines 304-307, 329). Mosh's own client feeds the four values from network object accessors:
   - set_local_frame_sent   ← net.get_sent_state_last()        → sent_states.back().num
   - set_local_frame_acked  ← network->get_sent_state_acked()  → sent_states.front().num
   - set_local_frame_late_acked ← network->get_latest_remote_state().state.get_echo_ack()
   - set_send_interval      ← network->send_interval()         → ceil(SRTT/2), clamped [20, 250] ms

3. send_interval() COMPUTATION (transportsender-impl.h line 63): `int SEND_INTERVAL = lrint(ceil(connection->get_SRTT() / 2.0))`. The claim's label "SRTT/2 estimate" is precisely correct. The value passed to PredictionEngine is a plain unsigned int milliseconds — no UDP type leaks through.

4. echo_ack ORIGIN (completeterminal.cc lines 73-116): echo_ack is carried in mosh's protobuf terminal-state messages (echoack extension). It represents the server acknowledging the frame sequence number of the last input it echoed. This is a logical concept portable to any sequenced transport.

5. SRTT ORIGIN (network.cc lines 503-519): SRTT is computed from timestamp_reply fields in mosh UDP packets using RFC 6298 (Jacobson/Karels). This is the only UDP-specific detail — but PredictionEngine receives it as a millisecond integer, decoupled from the measurement mechanism.

6. NOSSHTRADAMUS EMPIRICAL EVIDENCE (https://github.com/thyth/nosshtradamus): The project operates as an SSH/TCP proxy and re-implements the four variables via a "side-band ping" mechanism — sequential SSH ping requests written immediately after each keystroke, with ping response sequence numbers used to reconstruct frame-acked signals. The README confirms it uses mosh's prediction engine and terminal emulator components verbatim over TCP.

The claim's four named variables exactly match the four PredictionEngine member variables (local_frame_sent, local_frame_acked, local_frame_late_acked at terminaloverlay.h:238; send_interval at terminaloverlay.h:264). All four are injected through plain setters; the engine never touches the network object directly.
verdict.correction: No correction needed. The claim is accurate in all named details: the four variable names are exact, the SRTT/2 formula is confirmed (transportsender-impl.h line 63: ceil(SRTT/2.0) clamped to [20,250] ms), the echo_ack description matches completeterminal.cc, and nosshtradamus is a real project that empirically demonstrates the engine running over SSH/TCP. One nuance worth adding: SRTT is measured from UDP packet timestamps in mosh's own Connection class, but this is fully abstracted away — PredictionEngine only ever receives the derived millisecond integer, not any UDP-layer object.

rawCorpus.summary: SwiftTerm (migueldeicaza/SwiftTerm, current version v1.13.0, March 2026) is the clear recommendation for the client-side terminal renderer in this hybrid architecture. It is a native Swift, actively maintained library that covers the full xterm/VT500 escape sequence set, ships a Metal GPU-accelerated rendering path (macOS, iOS, visionOS), handles Unicode grapheme clusters and emoji correctly, has documented integration patterns for SSH/network streams, and is already deployed in production apps (Secure Shellfish, La Terminal, CodeEdit, a-Shell). Ligatures are NOT explicitly suppressed — CoreText renders whatever the font provides — which is a minor concern for cell-alignment but not a correctness blocker for a coding tool. The wiring pattern for our architecture is a thin adapter: network bytes from Network.framework arrive on a background queue, get handed to `terminalView.feed(byteArray:)`, and user keystrokes flow back via the `TerminalViewDelegate.send(source:data:)` callback to the PTY stdin of the host.

techniques.name: SwiftTerm TerminalView + Metal renderer
techniques.how: Embed TerminalView (NSView on macOS, UIView on iOS) from the SwiftTerm Swift Package. Call `terminalView.setUseMetal(true)` after the view is added to a window to activate the Metal path. Wire the data flow: implement `TerminalViewDelegate.send(source:data:)` to forward keystrokes to the transport layer; call `terminalView.feed(byteArray:)` when bytes arrive from the host's PTY stream. Handle window resize by calling `sshChannel.requestPtyResize(cols:rows:)` from `TerminalViewDelegate.sizeChanged(source:newCols:newRows:)`. Metal renderer uses a CoreText glyph atlas rasterized at `backingScaleFactor()` (2x on Retina, 3x on some iPads), cell origins are pixel-snapped with `ceil(dim * scale) / scale` to prevent sub-pixel seams.
techniques.impact: Zero additional serialization overhead on the text path — bytes from the transport drop directly into the emulator parser. Metal rendering reduces CPU load significantly vs CoreGraphics for large terminals or fast-updating screens. Correct cell dimensions on Retina and iPad Retina displays out of the box. No 4:4:4 concerns — this path bypasses video entirely.

techniques.name: Network stream adapter (no SSH library required)
techniques.how: SwiftTerm intentionally excludes an SSH library. The documented integration pattern for a custom transport is: implement `TerminalViewDelegate.send(source:data:)` to write bytes to the transport (our case: write to the NWConnection sending PTY stdin to the host), and on data receipt from NWConnection call `DispatchQueue.main.async { terminalView.feed(byteArray: slice) }`. The delegate callback `sizeChanged(source:newCols:newRows:)` propagates column/row changes back to the host so it can issue TIOCSWINSZ on the PTY. For SSH-style environment setup, `Terminal.getEnvironmentVariables(termName:trueColor:)` builds the correct TERM/COLORTERM env vars to advertise to the remote shell.
techniques.impact: Very low integration complexity. The transport is completely decoupled from the emulator — any byte stream source (NWConnection, WebSocket, Unix socket) works identically. Removes the CGEvent/Accessibility limitation for the terminal path entirely because input is plain bytes to the transport rather than synthetic OS-level events.

techniques.name: Retina / pixel-snapped cell grid
techniques.how: SwiftTerm's `computeFontDimensions()` calls `backingScaleFactor()` and snaps cell width and height: `snappedWidth = ceil(cellWidth * scale) / scale`, `snappedHeight = ceil(cellHeight * scale) / scale`. The Metal renderer reads `backingScaleFactor()` on iOS/visionOS and `metalRenderingScaleFactor()` on macOS, then sets `view.drawableSize = bounds * scale` each frame, so glyph atlas textures are always 1:1 with device pixels. On macOS, `layer.contentsScale` is updated to match the current display's scale factor each draw call, handling display migration (e.g. plugging in an external monitor).
techniques.impact: Crisp monospace text on all Retina and ProMotion displays without any per-app work. Avoids the fractional-pixel seams that cause blurry text in naive implementations.

techniques.name: Metal buffering mode tuning
techniques.how: Set `terminalView.metalBufferingMode` to control CPU/GPU tradeoff. `.perRowPersistent` (default) builds per-row MTLBuffers and caches them; only dirty rows are rebuilt. `.perFrameAggregated` rebuilds all visible rows into a single large buffer each frame, cheaper for full-screen TUI apps (vim, htop) that repaint most rows per frame. The row cache keys on `(lineRef identity, generation UInt64)` so unchanged lines are free. For the coding use-case where the screen is mostly static, `.perRowPersistent` is optimal — only the line being typed changes.
techniques.impact: Reduces GPU encode time and CPU work in the common case (mostly idle terminal). Relevant for battery life on iPad.

techniques.name: Kitty Keyboard Protocol (enhanced key reporting)
techniques.how: SwiftTerm implements the Kitty keyboard protocol (CSI u / progressive enhancement flags). The terminal tracks `keyboardEnhancementFlags` per-buffer. This is important for coding tools because it disambiguates key chords that are identical in legacy xterm encoding (e.g. Ctrl+Shift+letter, Escape vs CSI, Tab vs Ctrl+I). Neovim and many modern TUI editors request this mode. No host-side changes needed; the host PTY shell/app requests the flags via escape sequences and the client responds correctly.
techniques.impact: Full key fidelity for modern TUI coding editors (Neovim, Helix, etc.) over the remote PTY path.

techniques.name: Synchronized output (CSI 2026)
techniques.how: SwiftTerm implements DEC Synchronized Output (private mode 2026). When active, display updates are batched; a 1-second safety timeout auto-resets the synchronized state, and window resize also resets it. The delegate gets `synchronizedOutputChanged(source:active:)` callbacks. This eliminates the partial-frame flicker visible in high-throughput output (e.g. `make` output, `git log --graph`) where the terminal redraws mid-escape-sequence. This was implemented in SwiftTerm and is present in the current codebase.
techniques.impact: Crisp, flicker-free rendering during bursts of output — directly relevant to the coding use-case.

techniques.name: Sixel and Kitty graphics protocol
techniques.how: SwiftTerm supports both sixel (DCS passthrough via `SixelDcsHandler`, decodes to RGBA bitmap and calls `TerminalDelegate.createImageFromBitmap`) and the full Kitty graphics protocol (APC `G` command handler, supports direct/file/shared-memory transmission, cropping, virtual placements, PNG and raw RGBA formats, zlib compression). The Metal renderer caches Kitty image textures by imageId. iTerm2 inline image protocol (OSC 1337) is also parsed. For the coding use-case sixel/Kitty are rare but tools like `imgcat`, `chafa`, or `wezterm imgcat` work if needed.
techniques.impact: Complete graphics protocol support — not needed for daily coding but available without any host changes.

techniques.name: Blink Shell (alternative — hterm/JS-based, not Swift-native)
techniques.how: Blink Shell renders its terminal via Chromium's hterm (JavaScript), wrapped in a WKWebView on iOS. It is open source at github.com/blinksh/blink. For our architecture this is not suitable: the WKWebView bridge adds latency, JavaScript rendering is harder to customize for our LAN-only non-SSH use-case, and the Swift integration surface is much thinner than SwiftTerm's native UIKit/AppKit views.
techniques.impact: Not recommended for this hybrid architecture. Mentioned only for completeness as the main commercial alternative iOS terminal.

techniques.name: a-Shell custom terminal renderer (alternative — also uses SwiftTerm)
techniques.how: a-Shell, an iOS terminal emulator for local commands, migrated to SwiftTerm as its rendering system from an older custom renderer. This validates SwiftTerm's suitability for interactive, performance-sensitive terminal use on iOS. Not an alternative to SwiftTerm — it is a consumer of the same library.
techniques.impact: Production validation of SwiftTerm on iOS for interactive coding-class workloads.

key_settings: terminalView.setUseMetal(true) — opt-in to GPU rendering; falls back gracefully to CoreGraphics if Metal unavailable
terminalView.metalBufferingMode = .perRowPersistent — best for mostly-static coding terminal (default); switch to .perFrameAggregated for full-screen TUI apps
terminalView.font = NSFont(name: "YourFont", size: N) — monospaced font; cell dimensions auto-computed from 'W' glyph advance width on macOS, string size on iOS
terminalView.fontSmoothing (macOS only) — controls sub-pixel rendering; false = thin strokes matching iTerm2 'Thin strokes' setting
terminalView.lineSpacing — multiplier for vertical spacing; 1.0 default, 1.1 matches iTerm2 setting
Terminal.getEnvironmentVariables(termName: "xterm-256color", trueColor: true) — produces correct TERM/COLORTERM env to pass to remote shell on connect
TerminalOptions.scrollback — configure scrollback buffer size at Terminal init time

terminalView.customBlockGlyphs — when true, box-drawing (U+2500..U+257F) and block-element (U+2580..U+259F) characters are rendered by SwiftTerm's custom vector renderer rather than the font, ensuring pixel-perfect alignment regardless of font design
SWIFTTERM_PROFILE=1 environment variable — enables os_signpost profiling for Metal render pass timing
SWIFTTERM_METAL_LIVE_RESIZE_THROTTLE=0 — disables macOS live-resize throttling if immediate Metal redraws during resize are preferred
latency_numbers: feed(byteArray:) is thread-safe and can be called from any queue; the call itself is synchronous in the terminal model, display is queued via queuePendingDisplay() — no measured round-trip latency published in the repo
Metal row cache hit: unchanged rows cost ~0 GPU CPU work per frame (only dirty rows rebuilt); debug output format: 'Metal FPS: %.1f (rows rebuilt: %d/%d)' visible when SWIFTTERM_PROFILE=1
Cell dimension computation: glyph advance of 'W' measured once at font-set time; subsequent renders use cached cellDimension — no per-frame font measurement
Glyph atlas: CoreText rasterizes at backingScaleFactor (2x Retina); glyphs cached in MTLTexture atlas keyed by (fontName, size, CGGlyph); atlas auto-rebuilds and clears row cache on atlas resize
Synchronized output timeout: 1.0 second safety reset — means a hung CSI 2026 sequence that never closes will delay display by at most 1 second
Kitty image cache: up to 4 GB (kittyMaxImageCacheBytes = 4 * 1024 * 1024 * 1024), max image dimension 10,000px, max image bytes 400 MB per image
claims_to_verify: LOAD-BEARING: 'feed(byteArray:) can be invoked from a background thread' — the README and doc comment in AppleTerminalView.swift line ~1997 say this, but the delegate callbacks (queuePendingDisplay, etc.) go back to the main queue internally. Verify in a stress test that no UI mutation leaks to the background caller.

LOAD-BEARING: Ligatures — SwiftTerm's getAttributes() does NOT set NSLigatureAttributeName = 0. CoreText therefore renders whatever ligatures the font defines (e.g. Fira Code '->' as a single glyph). For a monospaced terminal this can break cell-column alignment. Verify with a ligature font before shipping; if it misaligns, add NSLigatureAttributeName: 0 to nsattr in getAttributedValue(). The Metal path rasterizes individual CGGlyphs via CTFontGetGlyphsForCharacters so ligature behavior there depends on how CoreText expands the ligature during glyph lookup — needs testing.

LOAD-BEARING: Emoji column width — Utilities.swift uses a static twoColumnEmoji table for width=2 classification. ZWJ sequences (family emoji, skin-tone modifiers) may be misclassified if they are not in the table, causing cursor drift. Recent fix mentioned in search results addressed this for common cases but the table is not auto-generated from Unicode data; it needs periodic updates.

Thread safety of Terminal object itself — Terminal.swift doc says 'Thread-safe Terminal instances'. Verify this applies to concurrent feed() calls from a receive loop vs main-thread delegate callbacks, or whether a single serial queue is still required.

Metal availability on older iOS hardware — setUseMetal(true) throws MetalError.deviceUnavailable on non-Metal hardware. The CoreGraphics fallback path is maintained and functional but has not been benchmarked against the Metal path for the typical coding workload.
visionOS support — MetalTerminalRenderer.swift has #if os(visionOS) guards; verify TerminalView layout in visionOS window context if iPadOS-as-visionOS deployment is in scope.
open_questions: Input method / soft keyboard on iPad: SwiftTerm's UIView needs UIKeyInput or UITextInput conformance to receive software keyboard events correctly. The iOS sample app (iOSTerminal) handles this, but verify special-key (arrow, function, Escape, modifier combos) encoding for coding tools on iPad without hardware keyboard.

Kitty keyboard protocol client-side encoding: SwiftTerm parses and tracks the server's keyboardEnhancementFlags, but does the iOS/macOS key-event → byte encoding side in TerminalView already emit Kitty-protocol-encoded sequences when flags are active? The host app (Neovim etc.) will request the mode; need to confirm SwiftTerm's key encoder sends the right CSI u sequences back.

Cursor blink timer on iOS: The Metal renderer's updateCursorBlinkTimer uses a Timer. On iOS, Timer delivery while the app is backgrounded or the screen is locked is unreliable. Need to verify cursor state is correct on app foreground resume.

Selection copy on iOS: AppleTerminalView handles clipboard copy via TerminalViewDelegate.clipboardCopy (writes to NSPasteboard on macOS). On iOS the delegate must handle UIKit pasteboard. The default implementation is not provided for iOS — the app must implement this.

Scrollback memory: TerminalOptions.scrollback controls the buffer. For a long-running coding session this can grow unbounded. No documented API for per-session scrollback cap or eviction policy beyond the initial setting.

macOS input injection bypass validation: The hybrid architecture's key architectural win is that the terminal path bypasses CGEvent injection by sending keystrokes as bytes to the PTY stdin over the network. This should be confirmed with a complete prototype — specifically that no CGEvent or Accessibility API calls are needed anywhere in the SwiftTerm key-handling path on the CLIENT side (the client never injects into the host OS; it only sends bytes via the transport).

verifications.claim: LOAD-BEARING: 'feed(byteArray:) can be invoked from a background thread' — the README and doc comment in AppleTerminalView.swift line ~1997 say this, but the delegate callbacks (queuePendingDisplay, etc.) go back to the main queue internally. Verify in a stress test that no UI mutation leaks to the background caller.

verdict.claim: feed(byteArray:) can be invoked from a background thread — the README and doc comment in AppleTerminalView.swift say this, but the delegate callbacks (queuePendingDisplay, etc.) go back to the main queue internally. No UI mutation leaks to the background caller.
verdict.evidence:
Direct source examination of the SwiftTerm clone at migueldeicaza/SwiftTerm confirms the following concrete facts:

1. The doc comment at [AppleTerminalView.swift line 1995](https://github.com/migueldeicaza/SwiftTerm/blob/main/Sources/SwiftTerm/Apple/AppleTerminalView.swift#L1995) reads exactly: "Sends data to the terminal emulator for interpretation, this can be invoked from a background thread". The README (line 72) says "Thread-safe Terminal instances" — but this refers to the lower-level Terminal engine class, not TerminalView.

2. The call chain from feed(byteArray:) is: feedPrepare() → terminal.feed(buffer:) → feedFinish() → queuePendingDisplay(). ALL of these run synchronously on whatever thread the caller is on.

3. queuePendingDisplay() (line 1775) reads and writes the unprotected var pendingDisplay: Bool (declared in MacTerminalView.swift line 118, no NSLock, no @MainActor, no actor isolation). It sets pendingDisplay = true on the background caller thread, then calls DispatchQueue.main.asyncAfter(execute: updateDisplay). The reset pendingDisplay = false runs in updateDisplay on the main queue. This is a classic unsynchronized read-modify-write across two threads — a data race on pendingDisplay.

4. feedPrepare() (lines 1978–1986) also directly mutates selection.active = false and calls search.invalidate() from the background caller thread, with no main-queue hop. These are UI-layer mutations, not just Terminal engine mutations.

5. The actual drawing calls (updateDisplay, setNeedsDisplay, CoreText/Metal rendering) are correctly dispatched to DispatchQueue.main via asyncAfter, so the paint path itself does not execute on the background thread.

6. In the built-in MacLocalTerminalView + LocalProcess usage (the most common path), LocalProcess.init defaults dispatchQueue to DispatchQueue.main (LocalProcess.swift line 115), so dataReceived → feed(byteArray:) is normally delivered on the main thread anyway — making the background-thread guarantee rarely exercised in practice.
verdict.correction:
The claim is partially correct but incomplete, which makes it load-bearing dangerous:

CORRECT: The doc comment does state background-thread invocability. CORRECT: updateDisplay (the actual paint) is dispatched to DispatchQueue.main, so drawing itself does not run on the background caller.

WRONG/INCOMPLETE: The claim says no UI mutation leaks to the background caller. In fact two UI-layer mutations DO run on the background caller thread synchronously inside feed(byteArray:): (a) selection.active = false and search.invalidate() inside feedPrepare(), and (b) a read-modify-write of the unprotected Bool pendingDisplay inside queuePendingDisplay(). There is no NSLock, @MainActor annotation, or serial queue protecting pendingDisplay between the background writer (feed path sets it true) and the main-queue reader/resetter (updateDisplay sets it false). This is a real data race.

PRACTICAL MITIGATION: The default LocalProcess setup dispatches dataReceived on DispatchQueue.main, so in the common built-in usage the background-thread path is never actually taken. Custom integrations that call feed(byteArray:) from a true background thread (e.g. a network SSH read loop) without hopping to main first should be treated as unsafe until the above races are fixed or the caller serializes onto main. The stress test the original claim recommends is warranted — it would surface the pendingDisplay race and potential crashes in feedPrepare().

verifications.claim: LOAD-BEARING: Ligatures — SwiftTerm's getAttributes() does NOT set NSLigatureAttributeName = 0. CoreText therefore renders whatever ligatures the font defines (e.g. Fira Code '->' as a single glyph). For a monospaced terminal this can break cell-column alignment. Verify with a ligature font before shipping; if it misaligns, add NSLigatureAttributeName: 0 to nsattr in getAttributedValue(). The Metal path rasterizes individual CGGlyphs via CTFontGetGlyphsForCharacters so ligature behavior there depends on how CoreText expands the ligature during glyph lookup — needs testing.

verdict.claim: SwiftTerm's getAttributes() does NOT set NSLigatureAttributeName = 0. CoreText therefore renders whatever ligatures the font defines (e.g. Fira Code '->' as a single glyph). For a monospaced terminal this can break cell-column alignment. The Metal path rasterizes individual CGGlyphs via CTFontGetGlyphsForCharacters so ligature behavior there depends on how CoreText expands the ligature during glyph lookup.
verdict.evidence:
AppKit/UIKit path — getAttributes() in AppleTerminalView.swift (lines 446–524) and getAttributedValue() (lines 380–424) both build nsattr with only .font, .foregroundColor, .backgroundColor, plus conditional underline/strikethrough keys. Neither sets .ligature (NSLigatureAttributeName). Source: https://github.com/migueldeicaza/SwiftTerm/blob/main/Sources/SwiftTerm/Apple/AppleTerminalView.swift lines 497–502 and 406–412.

Metal shaper path — ShaperCache.shape() in MetalTerminalRenderer.swift (lines 1795–1808) constructs NSAttributedString(string: text, attributes: [.font: font]) — font only, no ligature suppression — then calls CTLineCreateWithAttributedString + CTRunGetGlyphs. CoreText performs full OpenType feature processing (including ligature substitution) before returning CGGlyph arrays. A two-character sequence like '->' that the font maps to a single ligature glyph will produce one CGGlyph from CTRunGetGlyphs.

Cell alignment impact — the Metal glyph placement loop (lines ~1310–1360) positions glyph i of a run at column startColumn + (drawnGlyphsInRun + i) * columnWidth. If CoreText collapses two characters to one glyph, that glyph lands at the first cell; the second cell gets no glyph drawn, and the ligature glyph (designed with a single-cell advance) visually overflows into it. This confirms the cell-column alignment risk.

One claim correction: The Metal path does NOT use CTFontGetGlyphsForCharacters. The shaping step uses CTLineCreateWithAttributedString → CTRunGetGlyphs (in ShaperCache.shape), and the rasterization step uses CTFontDrawGlyphs on the already-resolved CGGlyph (in CoreTextGlyphRasterizer.rasterize). CTFontGetGlyphsForCharacters does not appear in the Metal rendering path at all.
verdict.correction: The core claim is correct — no NSLigatureAttributeName = 0 is set anywhere in either rendering path, so ligatures are active. The cell-alignment risk is real and confirmed by reading the per-glyph placement logic. One sub-claim is wrong: the Metal path does NOT use CTFontGetGlyphsForCharacters. The actual API chain is: (1) shaping — CTLineCreateWithAttributedString([.font: font]) → CTLineGetGlyphRuns → CTRunGetGlyphs, all inside ShaperCache.shape(); (2) rasterization — CTFontDrawGlyphs on a single pre-resolved CGGlyph in CoreTextGlyphRasterizer.rasterize(). CTFontGetGlyphsForCharacters is absent from the Metal path entirely. The fix advice remains valid: adding NSAttributedString.Key.ligature: 0 to the nsattr dictionary in both getAttributes() and in the NSAttributedString built inside ShaperCache.shape() would suppress ligatures on both paths.

verifications.claim: LOAD-BEARING: Emoji column width — Utilities.swift uses a static twoColumnEmoji table for width=2 classification. ZWJ sequences (family emoji, skin-tone modifiers) may be misclassified if they are not in the table, causing cursor drift. Recent fix mentioned in search results addressed this for common cases but the table is not auto-generated from Unicode data; it needs periodic updates.

verdict.claim: LOAD-BEARING: Emoji column width — Utilities.swift uses a static twoColumnEmoji table for width=2 classification. ZWJ sequences (family emoji, skin-tone modifiers) may be misclassified if they are not in the table, causing cursor drift. Recent fix mentioned in search results addressed this for common cases but the table is not auto-generated from Unicode data; it needs periodic updates.
verdict.evidence:
Direct source inspection of SwiftTerm HEAD (commit 9446f60) reveals three facts that collectively refute the claim:

1. twoColumnEmoji is dead code. The table is declared at Utilities.swift:123 as `private static let twoColumnEmoji: [LH]` but is never called anywhere in the codebase. A project-wide keyword search for `twoColumnEmoji` returns only the declaration line — no call site exists. The `columnWidth` function (Utilities.swift:323–379) does not invoke `bisearch` against `twoColumnEmoji`; it calls `isEastAsianWide` instead.

2. Emoji width is driven by an auto-generated table, not twoColumnEmoji. `columnWidth` delegates to `isEastAsianWide(irune)` (Utilities.swift:374), which queries `UnicodeWidthData.eastAsianWide`. That struct lives in `Sources/SwiftTerm/UnicodeWidthData.swift`, whose first line reads "// This file is generated from Unicode data files. Do not edit by hand." It is regenerated by `scripts/regen_unicode_width_data.py`, which fetches `https://www.unicode.org/Public/UCD/latest/ucd/EastAsianWidth.txt` and `emoji-variation-sequences.txt` directly from unicode.org. The claim that the width table "is not auto-generated from Unicode data" is precisely backwards.

3. ZWJ sequences are not classified by any table lookup. Terminal.swift:1295–1430 handles ZWJ combining dynamically: when the code sees a scalar whose `value == 0x200D` (ZWJ), or when the previous stored character ends with a ZWJ, `shouldTryCombine` is set to true and the new scalar is appended to the previous grapheme cluster in the buffer. The resulting width is inherited from the leading character's stored `cd.width`, not computed fresh from a table. Skin-tone modifiers (`isEmojiModifier == true`) are also caught by the `shouldTryCombine` flag (Terminal.swift:1303) and attached to the previous cell without a table lookup.
verdict.correction:
The corrected picture has three parts:

(a) twoColumnEmoji is vestigial dead code. It was presumably the original emoji-width mechanism but has been superseded and is no longer called. The comment in Utilities.swift (line 119–122) even acknowledges this approach was "probably the wrong approach." No cursor drift risk from twoColumnEmoji because it is not invoked.

(b) The active width-classification path for emoji is `isEastAsianWide` → `UnicodeWidthData.eastAsianWide`, which IS auto-generated by a Python script from the official Unicode EastAsianWidth.txt data file. The table does need periodic re-generation to stay current with new Unicode versions, but it is a generated artifact, not a hand-curated list. If it has not been regenerated recently, it may be behind the latest Unicode standard — that is a real but different concern from the claim's framing.

(c) ZWJ sequence handling in Terminal.swift is structural (grapheme-cluster combining logic), not table-driven. The width of a completed ZWJ sequence inherits from the leading character's already-stored width. There is a genuine residual concern: if the leading base emoji (e.g. a person emoji) itself gets an incorrect width from the EAW table for some reason, that error would propagate to the whole ZWJ sequence. However, this is an EAW-table-staleness risk, not a twoColumnEmoji classification problem.

rawCorpus.summary: The hybrid app has two sharply distinct paths: (1) a remote shell (PTY text path) where the host spawns a shell via openpty()/forkpty(), streams the raw byte stream to the client, and the client renders it with SwiftTerm — input is plain bytes written to PTY stdin, no macOS event injection needed; and (2) a GUI window video path where ScreenCaptureKit captures per-window frames, VideoToolbox encodes HEVC, the network carries the stream, and the client injects mouse/keyboard events back via CGEventPostToPid or the private SkyLight SLEventPostToPid SPI. The architectural split maps cleanly: the PTY path completely sidesteps the macOS CGEvent/Accessibility injection problem because no event injection is ever needed — keystrokes are just bytes written to a file descriptor. The GUI path retains the full injection complexity. Given that VS Code holds 75.9% IDE market share but Vim/Neovim together account for ~38% of developer tool usage (Stack Overflow 2025), and that terminal-centric workflows (Neovim + tmux, CLI tools, git, build systems) dominate daily coding on a remote Mac, a terminal-first architecture with video as an opt-in feature per-window is the correct product design. The recommendation is: ship PTY shell first (low risk, clean APIs, tiny bandwidth), add video mirroring as a secondary "mirror this window" feature launched on demand, and accept the CGEvent injection limitations with transparent fallbacks for non-terminal windows.

techniques.name: PTY remote shell via openpty() / forkpty()
techniques.how: Host calls Darwin.openpty() (preferred over posix_openpt() which is broken on macOS) to get master+slave fd pair, spawns shell with slave as controlling terminal, streams raw bytes from master fd to client. forkpty() is not safe to call from Swift directly — use openpty() + Process with fd attachment instead. SwiftTerm's LocalProcessTerminalView wraps this for the local case; for the remote case, the client implements TerminalViewDelegate and feeds the network byte stream into the SwiftTerm engine directly.
techniques.impact: Eliminates the entire CGEvent/Accessibility injection problem for terminal sessions. Input is bytes written to a network socket → written to PTY master fd on host. No Accessibility permission, no TCC, no activate-then-control, no AXUIElement mapping. Bandwidth: ~36–52 bytes per keystroke (raw byte: 1 byte + framing overhead), typical coding session output well under 10 kbps. Crisp text by construction — no video codec artifacts on terminal content.

techniques.name: SwiftTerm terminal emulator (client-side rendering)
techniques.how: Swift Package, UIKit (iOS/iPadOS) and AppKit (macOS) TerminalView implementations backed by a UI-agnostic VT100/xterm engine. For the remote PTY path: implement TerminalViewDelegate to feed bytes from the network connection into the engine; the engine processes escape sequences and updates the view. Optional GPU-accelerated Metal rendering on macOS/iOS/visionOS. The LocalProcessTerminalView handles the local PTY case; remote use requires the delegate pattern with an ssh/custom transport underneath.
techniques.impact: Full xterm/VT100 compatibility means any terminal app running on the remote shell (vim, neovim, tmux, htop, git TUI) renders correctly on iOS/iPadOS/macOS clients. Thread-safe engine. Metal rendering keeps CPU near zero on static terminal content. Used in production by Secure Shellfish, La Terminal, CodeEdit.

techniques.name: CGEventPostToPid keyboard injection (GUI window path)
techniques.how: CGEvent(keyboardEventSource:virtualKey:keyDown:) creates a synthetic key event; calling event.postToPid(pid) delivers it to the target process's event queue without entering the global HID stream (no cursor warp, no physical input side-effects). Requires the app to have Accessibility permission (kTCCServicePostEvent) from the user via System Settings > Privacy & Security > Accessibility. The host app must NOT be sandboxed for Accessibility to grant full CGEventPost rights; the Sandbox + temporary exception entitlements path is fragile.
techniques.impact: Works reliably for most native macOS apps (TextEdit, Terminal, most AppKit apps). Fails silently for Chromium-based apps (VS Code Electron renderer, Chrome, Slack) because the renderer IPC filter rejects synthesized events that lack hardware telemetry bytes (mouseEventSubtype, clickState counter, window-local coordinate stamp set by private SPI). Keyboard-only (as opposed to mouse) injection via postToPid is accepted by Electron/VS Code text areas in practice — the restriction is stricter for mouse events than keyboard events.

techniques.name: SkyLight private SPI (SLEventPostToPid) for Chromium/Electron injection
techniques.how: Discovered by grepping SkyLight symbol exports. No public header. Accessed via dlopen('/System/Library/PrivateFrameworks/SkyLight.framework/SkyLight', RTLD_NOW) + dlsym. Provides graceful degradation: if symbol disappears in a future macOS, fall back to CGEventPostToPid.
techniques.impact: Removes the Chromium/Electron rejection problem for mouse clicks. Still fails for: (1) right-clicks on web content (coerced to left-click at renderer IPC boundary), (2) canvas/game engine apps (Blender, Unity) that filter per-pid routes entirely and require brief foreground activation. Keyboard injection does not need SkyLight — CGEventPostToPid is sufficient for keyboard events in all tested apps including Electron. Private API: rejection risk on future macOS versions; no App Store distribution.

techniques.name: ScreenCaptureKit per-window capture (SCContentFilter desktopIndependentWindow)
techniques.how: SCContentFilter(desktopIndependentWindow: scWindow) creates a filter that captures exactly one window with no children, popups, or other windows included. SCStreamConfiguration.minimumFrameInterval controls max frame rate (e.g., CMTime(value:1, timescale:30) for 30fps). ScreenCaptureKit delivers frames via SCStreamOutput callback with an SCStreamFrameInfo.status field: .complete means new pixel data, .idle means no visual change since last frame — idle frames carry no new IOSurface. This enables encode-on-change: only encode+send when status == .complete. Frame delivery requires user permission prompt (Screen Recording TCC on macOS 12.3+, or the app must be on the exclusion list via SCShareableContent).
techniques.impact: Per-window capture at 30fps produces a stream that is effectively ~0 bandwidth when the IDE is static (only keystrokes happening, no repaints). On activity (typing with syntax highlighting, scrolling, autocomplete popups) it bursts. No cursor warp on the host side. Text in GUI windows is 4:2:0 HEVC — acceptable for reading code, slightly soft for sub-pixel rendering at high zoom, but the project has explicitly accepted this tradeoff.

techniques.name: VideoToolbox HEVC low-latency encode (kVTVideoEncoderSpecification_EnableLowLatencyRateControl)
techniques.how: Set kVTVideoEncoderSpecification_EnableLowLatencyRateControl = kCFBooleanTrue in the encoderSpecification dict passed to VTCompressionSessionCreate. On Intel Macs this flag only worked for H.264; on Apple Silicon it works for both H.264 and HEVC (confirmed via FFmpeg patch merged to master). Low-latency mode disables frame reordering (one-in, one-out), eliminating B-frame lookahead delay. Per Apple WWDC21 session 10158: reduces encode latency by up to 100ms at 720p 30fps vs default mode. For screen content (static large areas, sharp text edges), HEVC 4:2:0 at moderate bitrate (1–3 Mbps for 1080p window) is sufficient for legible text.
techniques.impact: Up to 100ms encode latency reduction (720p, 30fps). Hardware-accelerated on all Apple Silicon Macs and most Intel Macs with T2. The combination of SCK idle-frame skipping + VT low-latency encode means: during static coding sessions the encode pipeline is idle; on a keypress that triggers a repaint, the frame is captured and encoded within ~33ms (one frame interval) + encode overhead. Total host-side pipeline latency: capture + encode + packetize ≈ 50–80ms on Apple Silicon.

techniques.name: Activate-then-control pattern (fallback for canvas/game apps)
techniques.how: For apps that filter per-pid event routes entirely (Blender, Unity, games), the only reliable path is briefly bringing the target window to the foreground (NSApp.activate, or SLPSSetFrontProcessWithOptions), delivering the event, then returning focus. This steals the host desktop focus for ~1 frame. Acceptable for rare interactions; unacceptable for continuous typing.
techniques.impact: Breaks the no-focus-steal promise. Users on the host Mac see their active window briefly disappear. For the daily coding use case (VS Code, Xcode, browser) this path should be avoided entirely — use SLEventPostToPid or CGEventPostToPid instead. Reserve activate-then-control as an explicit fallback disclosed to the user.

techniques.name: Mosh-style local echo / speculative keystroke prediction (PTY path optimization)
techniques.how: Mosh's SSP protocol runs a client-side terminal emulator that predicts the result of the user's keystrokes and renders them immediately (underlined overlay), then confirms or cancels when the server's authoritative state arrives. The State Synchronization Protocol (UDP, AES-128 OCB3) synchronizes terminal framebuffer state objects rather than character streams — the server runs a full terminal emulator (Terminal::Framebuffer) and only sends diffs. Blink Shell implements this on iOS/iPadOS using libmosh.
techniques.impact: For LAN use (sub-1ms RTT), speculative echo is unnecessary — the round-trip is imperceptible. But mosh's architecture (state sync over UDP vs byte-stream over TCP) is worth knowing: for the LAN-only scope of this app, a simple TCP byte-stream PTY (Eternal Terminal style) is sufficient and simpler to implement than full SSP. Mosh's roaming is also irrelevant for LAN. For a simple first implementation, raw openpty() byte stream over a Network.framework NWConnection (TCP) is the correct starting point.
