open_questions: Is there a missing better approach: running VS Code Server natively on the Mac host and connecting the iPad to it over LAN (no relay, direct WebSocket) — this would give full VS Code semantics with no pixel streaming at all, and the hybrid tool could embed a WebView pointing at localhost:8080. Would this be better than building a custom PTY+video stack?

What is the right granularity for the window-type detector? The hybrid design needs to decide per-window whether to use PTY path or video path. How do existing tools (e.g., Apple's Accessibility framework, Instruments) classify 'is this window a terminal?' vs 'is this a GUI editor?'

For the video path: should the hybrid tool implement encode-on-change (only encode a new frame when the screen changes) or always-on low-bitrate? What is the ScreenCaptureKit API for content-change notification — does SCStreamConfiguration have a dirty-rect or motion threshold?

Does Blink Shell still use SwiftTerm as of 2025, or did they migrate to a different renderer? The search results were ambiguous on whether Blink currently ships SwiftTerm or still uses HTerm-derived rendering.

For LAN-only use: is mosh's UDP requirement (ports 60000–61000) a meaningful constraint if the app owns both host and client, or is a simple raw TCP PTY byte stream over Network.framework simpler and sufficient (no packet loss on LAN)?

verifications.claim: SwiftTerm throughput numbers appear inverted in the search result: '1,233 calls per second' vs '730k calls per second previously' — the readme likely states 730k→1,233k (millions), not hundreds. Verify against https://github.com/migueldeicaza/SwiftTerm README or CHANGELOG.

verdict.claim: SwiftTerm throughput numbers appear inverted in the search result: '1,233 calls per second' vs '730k calls per second previously' — the readme likely states 730k→1,233k (millions), not hundreds. Verify against https://github.com/migueldeicaza/SwiftTerm README or CHANGELOG.
verdict.evidence:
The current SwiftTerm README (278 lines, fetched via gh api) contains no throughput numbers at all — not "730k", not "1,233", not any calls/sec figure. The numbers exist only in git commit messages for two separate optimization commits:

Commit 5d814fc: "This goes from about 1,193k calls/sec to 1,233 call/sec on testPerformance2 on my Mac." — The "1,233" here is a dropped-k typo; context makes clear it means 1,233k calls/sec (the baseline in the same sentence is "1,193k calls/sec", so the result must also be in the thousands range). This is a ~3.3% improvement.

Commit 6cd178f: "Optimization work: Before: ~730K calls/s / After: ~770K calls/s" — This is a completely separate earlier optimization, improving an unsafe-array rewrite from 730K to 770K calls/s.

The claim conflates these two independent commits as if they were a before/after pair. They are not: 730K is an older baseline for a different optimization; 1,193k→1,233k is a later, separate optimization at a higher absolute throughput. There is no inversion. The direction is correct throughout (later = faster). The "1,233 calls per second" figure the claim flags as suspicious is indeed a missing-k typo in the commit message (should be 1,233k), but the numbers are not inverted and the README never contained these figures.
verdict.correction: The throughput numbers never appeared in the README — they exist only in git commit messages. The two figures (730K and 1,233) come from two separate optimization commits, not a single before/after. "1,233 call/sec" in commit 5d814fc is a typo for 1,233k calls/sec (the same commit's baseline is "1,193k calls/sec", confirming the unit). "730K calls/s" in commit 6cd178f is an earlier, unrelated optimization baseline. Neither number is inverted: all optimizations show improvement (730K→770K, then later 1,193K→1,233K). The correct statement is: SwiftTerm's benchmark commits show two separate throughput milestones — an earlier one at ~730K–770K calls/s and a later one at ~1,193K–1,233K calls/s — neither of which appears in the README.

verifications.claim: JetBrains RD protocol encryption transport: described as 'secure protocol under the hood' via SSH tunnel — verify whether it is vanilla SSH port-forwarding or a custom TLS layer on top.

verdict.claim: JetBrains RD protocol encryption transport is described as 'secure protocol under the hood' via SSH tunnel — verify whether it is vanilla SSH port-forwarding or a custom TLS layer on top.
verdict.evidence:
The claim presents a binary choice (SSH port-forwarding OR custom TLS), but the actual architecture is both layers simultaneously, explicitly documented and independently reverse-engineered.

1. Official JetBrains Security Model docs (identical across IntelliJ IDEA, Rider, PyCharm, CLion, GoLand) state verbatim: "The communication between JetBrains Client and the IDE backend is end-to-end encrypted with the 1.3 TLS even if performed in a secure SSH tunnel." This is a custom TLS 1.3 application-level layer stacked on top of SSH tunneling — neither alone.

2. The SSH side is vanilla SSH port-forwarding: the IDE backend on the remote host listens on a local port (typically 5990), and the JetBrains Client forwards that port via SSH to the local machine. The SSH implementation used is SSHJ 0.33. Network-level evidence from independent reverse engineering (wyh.life/article/2022/11/13/how-jetbrains-gateway-works) confirms: "TCP 127.0.0.1:55784->127.0.0.1:5990 (ESTABLISHED)" with sshd bridging through port 22 — textbook SSH port forwarding, not a custom tunnel daemon.

3. The TLS 1.3 layer is custom in the sense that it does NOT use root CA certificate hierarchies. Instead it uses: (a) a certificate fingerprint parameter (`fp`) embedded in the joinLink connection string, verified client-side against the server's self-signed cert, and (b) a one-time connection token (`jt`) that the server uses to reject unauthorized connections. The JSSE TLS debug log showing decrypted HEX dumps was confirmed by the reverse engineer.

4. The RD protocol itself (reactive distributed messaging framework, open-sourced at github.com/JetBrains/rd) rides inside this TLS socket. Protocol.cs shows IWire abstraction — TLS is the wire transport, RD messages are the payload.

Summary of stack (innermost to outermost): RD reactive protocol messages → TLS 1.3 socket (custom cert-pinning, no CA trust) → SSH port-forward tunnel → TCP.
verdict.correction: The claim ("SSH tunnel — verify whether vanilla SSH port-forwarding or a custom TLS layer on top") frames a false dilemma. The correct answer is: it is BOTH. The outer transport is vanilla SSH port-forwarding (using SSHJ 0.33). Inside that SSH tunnel, JetBrains adds a TLS 1.3 application-level layer with custom trust (cert fingerprint pinning + one-time token), not relying on any CA hierarchy. The RD reactive-distributed-communication protocol messages ride inside the TLS layer. So "SSH tunnel with a custom TLS layer on top" is the accurate description — and the official docs confirm this explicitly with the phrase "encrypted with TLS 1.3 even if performed in a secure SSH tunnel."

verifications.claim: VS Code Remote SSH binary wire protocol version uses the VS Code commit hash as version ID — verify this is still current in VS Code 1.99+ (March 2025 changed glibc requirements; protocol versioning may have changed too).

verdict.claim: VS Code Remote SSH binary wire protocol version uses the VS Code commit hash as version ID — this is still current in VS Code 1.99+ (March 2025 changed glibc requirements; protocol versioning may have changed too).
verdict.correction: The claim is accurate for the classic server handshake path and should be scoped more precisely: (1) The classic `ConnectionTypeRequest` WebSocket handshake continues to use the commit SHA as an exact-match version ID (`rendererCommit !== myCommit` → reject). This is still current in VS Code 1.99+. (2) VS Code 1.99+ adds a second Agent Host Protocol layer that uses SemVer `0.3.0`, not commit hashes. (3) The glibc ≥ 2.28 change in 1.99 did not affect protocol versioning. The claim should be narrowed: "the classic management/extension-host WebSocket handshake still uses the commit hash as version ID; the new Agent Host Protocol uses SemVer."

gapCorpus.summary: Clipboard sync splits cleanly along the two paths. The terminal path has a complete in-band mechanism (OSC 52) that SwiftTerm fully parses — both write (clipboardCopy delegate) and read/query (clipboardRead delegate, default nil/deny) — and that tmux/Neovim/shells can be configured to emit. The GUI video path has no in-band channel at all and requires an explicit out-of-band control-channel subprotocol modelled on RDP CLIPRDR's lazy-fetch design: host polls NSPasteboard.changeCount, client observes UIPasteboard.changedNotification, both announce formats then fetch on demand. The hardest problems are (1) OSC 52 read is a practical freeze risk in Neovim unless the client implements prompt response, (2) NSPasteboard has no push notification — polling at ~500 ms is the industry norm, (3) large payloads (>100 KB) need chunking on both paths, and (4) loop avoidance requires a generation/owner token so each side ignores changes it caused.

techniques.name: OSC 52 write (set) — terminal path
techniques.how: Remote app (Neovim, shell script, tmux) emits ESC ] 52 ; <sel> ; <base64-data> BEL. SwiftTerm's EscapeSequenceParser dispatches OSC code 52 to Terminal.oscClipboard(), which splits on the semicolon, base64-decodes the payload, and calls TerminalDelegate.clipboardCopy(source:content:Data). MacLocalTerminalView's default implementation calls NSPasteboard.general.clearContents() then writeObjects([str as NSString]). On iOS the default iOSTerminalView.clipboardCopy is a no-op stub — the app must override TerminalViewDelegate.clipboardCopy to write UIPasteboard.general.string = str.
techniques.impact: Zero extra protocol overhead. Works entirely inside the existing PTY byte stream. Host→client clipboard is essentially free once configured.

techniques.name: OSC 52 read (query) — terminal path, client→host
techniques.how: Remote app emits ESC ] 52 ; <sel> ; ? BEL. SwiftTerm.oscClipboard() detects the '?' payload and calls TerminalDelegate.clipboardRead(source:) -> Data?. If the delegate returns non-nil Data, SwiftTerm calls sendResponse(cc.OSC, "52;\(selectionChars);\(base64)", cc.ST) which sends the response back through the PTY to stdin of the remote process. The default TerminalDelegate.clipboardRead returns nil (deny). The app must implement clipboardRead to read UIPasteboard.general and return UTF-8 Data. In Neovim, the paste side of vim.g.clipboard = 'osc52' issues this query; many terminals deny it, causing a ~10 s freeze waiting for a response — prompt response from SwiftTerm is critical.
techniques.impact: Enables client→host paste over PTY with no extra channel. But if the client's clipboardRead is slow or times out, Neovim blocks the UI. Must respond within ~1 s or Neovim shows 'Waiting for OSC 52 response…'.

techniques.name: OSC 52 selection parameters
techniques.how: The <sel> field before the semicolon-separated base64 payload selects which clipboard/selection to target. Defined parameters: 'c' = clipboard (system clipboard, default if field is empty), 'p' = primary X selection, 's' = secondary X selection, '0'/'1' = cut buffers 0 and 1, 'q'/'k'/'d' = additional named clipboards. SwiftTerm's oscClipboard() defaults empty sel to 'c'. On macOS/iOS only 'c' is meaningful — NSPasteboard.general / UIPasteboard.general. Other parameters can be silently mapped to 'c' or ignored.
techniques.impact: For the hybrid tool only 'c' matters. Other params can be ignored or aliased to system clipboard with no loss of daily-coding functionality.

techniques.name: tmux set-clipboard configuration
techniques.how: tmux intercepts OSC 52 sequences from pane contents and (a) stores them in tmux paste buffers and (b) re-emits the OSC 52 sequence up to the outer terminal. The set-clipboard option controls this: 'on' = tmux sets clipboard AND allows inner apps to set it; 'external' (default since tmux 2.6) = tmux sets clipboard but inner apps cannot; 'off' = disabled. Requires the 'Ms' terminfo capability in the $TERM entry. Verify with 'tmux info | grep Ms:'. Add if missing via: set -as terminal-features ',xterm-256color:clipboard' (tmux 3.2+) or set -as terminal-overrides ',xterm-256color:Ms=\E]52;%p1%s;%p2%s\007'. For tmux >= 3.3a, inner apps can use 'set -g allow-passthrough on' to bypass tmux and emit OSC 52 directly to the outer terminal.
techniques.impact: Without set-clipboard on/external, yanks inside tmux sessions never reach the client clipboard. This is the most common misconfiguration in remote dev setups.

techniques.name: Neovim OSC 52 clipboard configuration
techniques.how: Neovim 0.10+ has built-in OSC 52 support. Shorthand: vim.g.clipboard = 'osc52'. Full form sets copy and paste separately via require('vim.ui.clipboard.osc52').copy('+') / .paste('+'). Neovim auto-detects terminal OSC 52 support and uses it as clipboard provider when 'clipboard' is unset and no other tool is found. Copy (write) works reliably. Paste (read via OSC 52 query) depends on terminal response — Neovim waits for a response and shows 'Waiting for OSC 52 response…' if it takes > ~1 s. Practical config for the hybrid tool: enable copy unconditionally, enable paste only if the SwiftTerm client implements prompt clipboardRead response. Pre-0.10 Neovim needs the nvim-osc52 plugin (now deprecated).
techniques.impact: Covers the dominant remote editor use-case. Copy path is zero-config after vim.g.clipboard = 'osc52'. Paste path requires client-side implementation quality.

techniques.name: OSC 52 size limits
techniques.how: No authoritative spec defines a maximum. Practical per-emulator limits vary widely: hterm originally 256 bytes (now higher), Tabby 1024 bytes (bug), st originally 382 bytes (now configurable up to 43 KB+), kitty had a 6138-byte limit (reported in issue #3031, now higher). xterm has no hard-coded limit and has been tested with 1 MB payloads. The base64 encoding adds ~33% overhead: 74,994 bytes of raw text base64-encodes to ~99,992 bytes of payload. For the hybrid tool (SwiftTerm is the outer terminal) the limit is whatever the app implements — there is no hardcoded cap in SwiftTerm. For large multi-MB code blocks, OSC 52 is risky over tmux (tmux buffers the entire sequence in memory before passing through). Pragmatic strategy: OSC 52 for ≤ 1 MB; for larger, use the explicit control-channel subprotocol.
techniques.impact: Most daily-coding yanks (function bodies, file paths, URLs) are well under 10 KB. Large copy operations (entire files) need a size gate or chunking strategy.

techniques.name: OSC 52 security — clipboard read exfiltration
techniques.how: The read/query direction (ESC ] 52 ; c ; ? BEL) lets a remote process read the local clipboard. This is a classic exfiltration vector: a malicious program on the host can silently read the client's clipboard (passwords, tokens, secrets copied locally). xterm disables OSC 52 read by default via disallowedWindowOps; to enable: XTerm*disallowedWindowOps: 20,21,SetXprop in .Xresources. Kitty: read-clipboard and read-primary must be explicitly added to clipboard_control option. tmux: the multi-client ambiguity also serves as a practical barrier. SwiftTerm's default clipboardRead returns nil (deny). Design implication: the hybrid tool should present a user-visible permission prompt before allowing clipboardRead to return real data — 'The remote host wants to read your clipboard. Allow?' — and should default to deny. The write direction is generally considered safe (host sets client clipboard) but note that a compromised host can overwrite the client clipboard with adversarial content (e.g. rewrite a pasted shell command).
techniques.impact: Security-critical. Default-deny for reads is correct. Write should be gated by user preference (auto-sync on/off toggle). Both directions should be disableable.

techniques.name: GUI video path — NSPasteboard polling on macOS host
techniques.how: NSPasteboard does not post a push notification for clipboard changes. The standard pattern (used by Maccy, Clipy, and other clipboard managers) is to poll NSPasteboard.general.changeCount on a timer. If changeCount differs from the last recorded value, read the new contents. Maccy polls at a configurable interval (default ~500 ms, configurable via UserDefaults). changeCount is an Int that monotonically increases with each write to the pasteboard. The sequence: timer fires → read changeCount → if changed, call NSPasteboard.general.data(forType:) for each relevant UTType → serialize → send over control channel. To avoid echo loops: after the client writes to the host clipboard (paste from client), the host records the changeCount of that write and skips sending it back.
techniques.impact: 500 ms polling latency is imperceptible for copy-paste in coding workflows. The changeCount trick is robust against race conditions.

techniques.name: GUI video path — UIPasteboard notification on iOS/iPadOS client
techniques.how: iOS/iPadOS provides UIPasteboard.changedNotification (NSNotification.Name) and UIPasteboard.removedNotification. These are push notifications posted to NotificationCenter when pasteboard contents change. Subscribe with NotificationCenter.default.addObserver(forName: UIPasteboard.changedNotification, object: UIPasteboard.general, queue: .main) { _ in ... }. Access content via UIPasteboard.general.string, .image, .url, .items, or .itemProviders. Privacy: iOS 14+ shows a banner when any app reads UIPasteboard unless the read is triggered directly by a user action. Background apps may not receive the notification reliably — test whether backgrounded client receives changedNotification.
techniques.impact: Push-based on iOS is cleaner than polling. The iOS 14 privacy banner is a UX issue to design around (explain to the user why the app reads the clipboard).

techniques.name: GUI path clipboard-sync subprotocol — RDP CLIPRDR lazy-fetch model
techniques.how: RDP CLIPRDR (MS-RDPECLIP, rev 16.0, April 2024) uses a delayed-rendering model designed for exactly this problem: (1) When clipboard changes on side A, A sends a FORMAT_LIST PDU announcing available types (CF_TEXT, CF_UNICODETEXT, CF_DIB, custom formats) but NOT the data. (2) Side B acknowledges with FORMAT_LIST_RESPONSE. (3) When a user on B pastes, B sends FORMAT_DATA_REQUEST for the specific type it needs. (4) A responds with FORMAT_DATA_RESPONSE containing the actual bytes. This avoids sending large payloads speculatively. For the hybrid tool's control channel: (1) Host detects NSPasteboard changeCount delta → sends CLIP_ANNOUNCE{changeId, formats:[{type, name}]} over control connection. (2) Client receives CLIP_ANNOUNCE → stores it, optionally writes a sentinel to UIPasteboard. (3) User pastes on client → client sends CLIP_FETCH{changeId, type} → host responds with CLIP_DATA{changeId, type, bytes}. Loop avoidance: each side tracks the changeId of the last clipboard it pushed; if it receives an announce with its own changeId, it ignores it.
techniques.impact: Lazy fetch avoids sending multi-MB payloads the user never pastes. The changeId prevents infinite echo loops. This is the architecturally correct design for the GUI path.

techniques.name: Multi-type pasteboard handling (plain text, RTF, images, file URLs)
techniques.how: NSPasteboard supports writing multiple representations in one transaction: call clearContents(), then prepare an array of NSPasteboardItem objects each declaring multiple types via setData(_:forType:). Relevant UTTypes: .utf8PlainText (kUTTypeUTF8PlainText, 'public.utf8-plain-text'), .rtf ('public.rtf'), .png / .tiff for images, .fileURL ('public.file-url'). For the control channel, the FORMAT_LIST step should enumerate all types present in the pasteboard (not just string), allowing the client to request the richest format it can handle. On iOS, UIPasteboard.items is an array of [String: Any] where keys are UTType strings. For coding workflows, plain text is 95% of cases; RTF and images are rare but important for design docs. File URLs should likely be blocked or require explicit user confirmation (drag-and-drop surface is different from clipboard).
techniques.impact: Advertising multiple types allows richer paste targets (paste into Xcode rich text field gets RTF, paste into terminal gets plain text). Minimal extra complexity for the FORMAT_LIST step.

techniques.name: Cross-path clipboard bridge (terminal OSC 52 ↔ GUI video path)
techniques.how: When the user is in the GUI video path (VS Code window) and copies there, the copy goes to NSPasteboard via the host OS. The host's clipboard monitor detects the changeCount delta and sends CLIP_ANNOUNCE to the client — this is the same path as the pure GUI path. When the user is in the terminal path and the remote app emits OSC 52, SwiftTerm calls clipboardCopy → writes UIPasteboard. To make this also visible on the host (so the user can paste in a GUI window), the client must send a CLIP_ANNOUNCE back to the host over the control channel. The host then writes the OSC 52 content to NSPasteboard. The critical invariant: every clipboard write on either side, regardless of how it was triggered, eventually propagates to the other side via the control channel, with loop-breaking via generation tokens.
techniques.impact: Without the cross-path bridge, copy in terminal cannot paste in GUI window and vice versa — a critical daily-coding failure mode. The bridge requires exactly one extra message per copy event.

techniques.name: Loop avoidance — generation / owner token pattern
techniques.how: The standard approach (used by RDP CLIPRDR and clipboard managers like Maccy): each clipboard write is tagged with an owner identifier (e.g. a UUID or monotonic counter). When side A writes the clipboard and sends CLIP_ANNOUNCE{changeId='x', owner='A'}, side B receives it, writes its local clipboard with a marker, and suppresses the resulting local change notification because it was caused by the remote announce. Maccy implements this by checking for a '.fromMaccy' marker string appended to pasteboard items. For the hybrid tool: the host records the changeCount value of every pasteboard write it performed in response to a client announce; when the timer fires and detects that changeCount delta, it checks if it was self-caused and skips sending. On iOS, the client records the last changeId it applied and ignores UIPasteboard.changedNotification if the change matches that ID.
techniques.impact: Without loop avoidance, a single copy event generates infinite CLIP_ANNOUNCE ping-pong across the control channel.

techniques.name: tmux pasting from system clipboard to remote session
techniques.how: tmux's paste direction is asymmetric: tmux will push copies outward (to system clipboard) but pulling from system clipboard into a tmux buffer requires an explicit command. The recommended mechanism: run 'tmux refresh-client -l' which syncs the current terminal's system clipboard into a tmux paste buffer (buffer index 0). This can be bound to a key: bind-key -T copy-mode-vi y send-keys -X copy-pipe-and-cancel 'tmux set-buffer - \; tmux refresh-client -l'. Alternatively, clients using xterm's bracketed paste mode (ESC[?2004h) can inject text directly into the PTY stdin as a bracketed paste, bypassing tmux clipboard entirely. For the hybrid tool's client: when the user triggers 'paste' in the SwiftTerm view, read UIPasteboard.general.string and send it as a bracketed paste sequence (ESC[200~ <text> ESC[201~) to PTY stdin — no OSC 52 query needed.
techniques.impact: Bracketed paste from client is simpler and more reliable than OSC 52 query for the paste direction. No freeze risk, no tmux configuration dependency.

key_settings: SwiftTerm TerminalViewDelegate.clipboardCopy(source:content:Data) — override on iOS to write UIPasteboard.general.string (default impl is no-op stub in iOSTerminalView)

SwiftTerm TerminalViewDelegate.clipboardRead(source:) -> Data? — override to read UIPasteboard.general and return UTF-8 Data; default returns nil (deny); must respond promptly (<1 s) to avoid Neovim freeze

tmux: set -s set-clipboard external in ~/.tmux.conf; verify with 'tmux info | grep Ms:'; add Ms capability via set -as terminal-features ',<TERM>:clipboard' if missing (tmux 3.2+); for tmux >= 3.3a use set -g allow-passthrough on for inner apps

Neovim: vim.g.clipboard = 'osc52' (shorthand, Neovim 0.10+); or explicit form with require('vim.ui.clipboard.osc52').copy/paste; enable paste direction only if client implements prompt clipboardRead response
OSC 52 size gate: send via OSC 52 if payload <= 1 MB (base64-encoded <= ~1.33 MB); route through control-channel CLIP_DATA for larger payloads
NSPasteboard polling: timer at ~500 ms calling NSPasteboard.general.changeCount; record last-seen changeCount to detect deltas; record self-written changeCount values to suppress echo
UIPasteboard notification: NotificationCenter.default.addObserver(forName: UIPasteboard.changedNotification, object: UIPasteboard.general, queue: .main)
Clipboard-sync security: default-deny clipboardRead; present permission prompt for first read; provide global on/off toggle for clipboard auto-sync; consider per-host trust setting
Loop avoidance: tag each clipboard write with a generation UUID; suppress local change detection when the detected changeCount matches a self-written value
latency_numbers: NSPasteboard polling at 500 ms: clipboard propagation latency to client is 0–500 ms depending on timer phase — imperceptible for copy-paste in coding
OSC 52 write latency: zero extra RTT — the escape sequence is embedded in the existing PTY byte stream; latency equals PTY stream latency (typically <10 ms on LAN)

OSC 52 read (query) latency: one full RTT (client receives query, reads UIPasteboard, sends response) plus Neovim's internal timeout; must complete in <1 s or Neovim shows waiting message and may freeze for ~10 s
RDP CLIPRDR lazy fetch adds one RTT for FORMAT_LIST + one RTT for DATA_REQUEST when paste occurs; for LAN (<1 ms RTT) this is imperceptible
UIPasteboard.changedNotification: push-based, fires within one runloop cycle of the write — effectively 0 ms detection latency on iOS

The Sourcegraph search found it in the current main branch (Tests/SwiftTermTests/OscTests.swift includes testOscClipboardReadDenied) but the release tag version is unknown.
