gapCorpus.dimension: Security architecture for a LAN-only native-Swift remote-shell and screen-control tool (macOS host spawns PTY shell; macOS/iOS clients connect)
gapCorpus.summary: A LAN-only remote-shell tool that writes arbitrary bytes to a PTY stdin is functionally equivalent to unauthenticated RCE on the host. "LAN-only" is not a security boundary: ARP spoofing, evil-twin APs, and compromised IoT devices on the same subnet all place an attacker in a full MITM position against plain TCP. The minimum viable security stack requires: (1) mutual authentication so only the paired client can connect, (2) transport encryption so a network-positioned attacker learns nothing, and (3) a trust-establishment ceremony that does not rely on a PKI the devices don't have. On Apple platforms, Network.framework TLS with self-signed identities plus sec_protocol_options_set_verify_block is the most native path; a custom Noise_XX handshake built on CryptoKit is a lighter-weight alternative with cleaner forward secrecy semantics. The Secure Enclave's SecureEnclave.P256.KeyAgreement.PrivateKey provides hardware-backed key storage on supported hardware (A7+ iPhone/iPad, T1/T2/Apple Silicon Mac) — the private key never leaves the chip. Pairing must be done out-of-band via a short authentication string or QR code so TOFU's first-connection window is eliminated. The Eternal Terminal CVE record and ET's SSH-bootstrapped passkey model are the clearest prior-art examples of what happens when these layers are absent or incorrectly layered.

techniques.name: Network.framework TLS with mutual self-signed identities (NWProtocolTLS + sec_protocol_options_set_verify_block)
techniques.how: Host and client each generate a Curve25519 or P256 key pair and a self-signed X.509 certificate at first launch. Both identities are stored in the Keychain (SecItemAdd with kSecAttrAccessibleWhenUnlockedThisDeviceOnly). The host configures an NWListener with NWProtocolTLS.Options; the client configures an NWConnection with TLS. Both sides call sec_protocol_options_set_local_identity(opts, sec_identity_create(cert, key)) to present their certificate. Both sides call sec_protocol_options_set_verify_block to reject any peer certificate whose SHA-256-of-DER hash does not match the stored 'known devices' list — this is the certificate-pinning step. sec_protocol_options_set_peer_authentication_required(opts, true) on the listener forces the client to present a certificate. After pairing (see SAS/QR technique below), the peer's certificate hash is stored permanently in the Keychain. This replaces system CA validation entirely for this peer-to-peer use case.
techniques.impact: Full transport encryption (TLS 1.3 with ECDHE + AES-256-GCM or ChaCha20-Poly1305 depending on negotiation) and mutual authentication. An attacker on the LAN can observe ciphertext only. Certificate pinning means a MITM cannot present a forged certificate. Eliminates the plain-TCP attack surface entirely. Native Apple API — no third-party dependencies. Drawback: TLS adds ~1 RTT handshake overhead per new connection; session resumption (TLS session tickets) available to amortize for reconnects.

techniques.name: CryptoKit custom secure channel: Curve25519 ECDH + HKDF + AES-GCM (Noise-inspired, no TLS)
techniques.how: Both sides generate a Curve25519.KeyAgreement.PrivateKey (CryptoKit). On connection, each sends its static public key bytes, derives a shared secret via sharedSecretFromKeyAgreement, stretches it with HKDF<SHA256>.deriveKey(inputKeyMaterial:salt:info:outputByteCount:) to produce two 256-bit AES-GCM keys (one per direction). Each message is sealed with AES.GCM.seal(plaintext, using: key, nonce: nonce) where nonce is a 96-bit monotonic counter. Authentication: each side signs their ephemeral public key with their long-term Ed25519 (Curve25519.Signing) key so the peer can verify identity before sending plaintext. This is essentially Noise_IK/XX built manually on CryptoKit primitives. For forward secrecy, re-run the ECDH step per session (new ephemeral keys each connection). Long-term static keys persist in Keychain.
techniques.impact: No TLS overhead, fully controllable framing, natural fit for binary PTY byte-stream. CryptoKit is ~constant-time and hardware-accelerated. Nonce counters give message ordering and anti-replay. Avoids Security.framework complexity (no X.509, no ASN.1). Drawback: requires implementing the handshake state machine correctly — an implementation error here is ship-stopping. More complex to audit than TLS. No session resumption built-in.

techniques.name: Noise Protocol Framework (Noise_XX or Noise_IK) via Swift library (OuterCorner/Noise or nixberg/noise-swift)
techniques.how: Noise_XX pattern: both sides start with only their own long-term static key. Handshake: -> e / <- e, ee, s, es / -> s, se. After 3 messages both sides have mutual authentication and a transport CipherState pair (one for each direction). Noise_IK: initiator already knows the responder's static public key (obtained at pairing time). Handshake: <- s (pre-message) / -> e, es, s, ss / <- e, ee, se. Zero-RTT first message possible but has weaker forward secrecy for that first message. OuterCorner/Noise wraps the noise-c C library in an Objective-C/Swift interface; nixberg/noise-swift is a pure Swift implementation using CryptoKit primitives. Recommended cipher suite: Noise_XX_25519_AESGCM_SHA256 or Noise_XX_25519_ChaChaPoly_SHA256.
techniques.impact: Noise provides the cleanest formal security properties of the three options: mutual authentication, identity hiding of both parties, strong forward secrecy, resistance to key-compromise impersonation. Noise_IK used by WireGuard and Tailscale's control plane in production. Drawback: the Swift libraries are community-maintained, not Apple-official; dependency risk. OuterCorner/Noise has a C library dependency (noise-c). Not a deferred tool.

techniques.name: Short Authentication String (SAS) / numeric-code out-of-band pairing ceremony
techniques.how: After the initial ECDH exchange (during pairing, not session connect), both sides derive a 4-6 digit decimal confirmation code by applying HKDF to the shared secret with a well-known info string, then truncating the output modulo 10^6 (or encoding as emoji/words). The host displays this code; the user visually verifies it matches on both devices before tapping 'Confirm'. Upon confirmation each side stores the peer's static public key in the Keychain tagged with a device name. This is the same mechanism used by Syncthing (abbreviated device ID + PIN exchange), Bluetooth Numeric Comparison (Secure Simple Pairing), and Signal's safety number ceremony. The confirmation window eliminates TOFU's 'leap of faith' vulnerability: a MITM substituting their key would produce a different SAS that the user would notice.
techniques.impact: Eliminates the first-connection MITM window without requiring a PKI or prior shared secret. One-time ceremony per device pair. After pairing, every subsequent connection verifies the pinned key cryptographically — no user involvement. Complexity: the host must display the code on a visible surface (macOS UI, menu bar popover) and the client must show the same code before the user taps confirm.

techniques.name: Secure Enclave key generation for device identity (SecureEnclave.P256.KeyAgreement.PrivateKey)
techniques.how: On supported hardware (A7+ iOS/iPadOS, T1/T2/Apple Silicon Mac), generate the device identity key pair entirely inside the Secure Enclave: let key = try SecureEnclave.P256.KeyAgreement.PrivateKey(). The private key never leaves the chip. To persist across app launches, save key.dataRepresentation (an opaque encrypted blob that only the same Secure Enclave can unwrap) to the Keychain: SecItemAdd with kSecAttrAccessibleWhenUnlockedThisDeviceOnly. To restore: SecureEnclave.P256.KeyAgreement.PrivateKey(dataRepresentation: stored). The public key is exported normally for exchange during pairing. Key agreement: key.sharedSecretFromKeyAgreement(with: peerPublicKey). Note: Secure Enclave supports P256 only (not Curve25519). If a Noise or CryptoKit Curve25519 channel is desired, the device identity signing key (for authentication) can live in the Secure Enclave (P256 ECDSA), while a separate ephemeral Curve25519 key handles the ECDH for each session — this is a valid and common split.
techniques.impact: Host's private key extraction is physically impossible even with root access or DMA attacks if generated in the Secure Enclave. Raises the bar from 'steal the key file' to 'physically compromise the Secure Enclave.' Limitation: P256 only; not available on older Macs without T-chip. dataRepresentation is device-specific — key does not survive OS reinstall or migration, requiring re-pairing. LAContext can gate key use on biometric authentication if desired.

techniques.name: Syncthing-pattern: device ID = SHA-256(DER certificate) used as mutual auth token
techniques.how: Each device generates a self-signed TLS certificate (ECDSA P384 or similar). The 'device ID' is SHA-256 of the certificate's DER-encoded bytes, base32-encoded into a human-readable string. At pairing, users exchange device IDs out-of-band (QR scan, copy-paste, or SAS comparison). On every TLS connection, each side receives the peer's certificate from the TLS handshake (cs.PeerCertificates[0]) and independently recomputes its SHA-256 hash, comparing against the stored device ID. If it does not match the authorized list, the connection is dropped immediately. Syncthing source (lib/connections/service.go:handleConns) shows: remoteID := protocol.NewDeviceID(remoteCert.Raw); if s.cfg.IgnoredDevice(remoteID) { c.Close() }. This pattern requires no PKI and no CA — the certificate is purely a transport for the public key.
techniques.impact: Simple, auditable, uses standard TLS for transport encryption while avoiding the CA trust model entirely. The device ID is short enough to display as a QR code or read aloud. Well-proven in production (Syncthing). The host maintains a whitelist of authorized device IDs; any client presenting an unknown certificate is dropped before any data is exchanged. Compatible with Network.framework TLS via sec_protocol_options_set_verify_block.

techniques.name: Eternal Terminal SSH-bootstrapped passkey model (and its failure modes)
techniques.how: ET generates a random 32-character alphanumeric passkey and 16-character session ID at connection setup time (SshSetupHandler.cpp:genCommand). The passkey is sent to the server via the SSH channel ('echo ID/PASSKEY | etterminal') and echoed back as IDPASSKEY: in stdout. The ET client then connects to the ET TCP port and presents the ID/passkey in the InitialPayload protobuf message. The server verifies passkey match. The channel between SSH and ET is the inherited TTY stdio — passkey is transmitted as cleartext on the SSH session's encrypted channel. Critically: (1) the ET TCP port has no transport encryption — the session after the passkey check is plaintext. (2) The 2021 CVE (GHSA-hxg8-4r3q-p9rv) showed that 'authenticated' did not prevent path-traversal + symlink race attacks that escalated to root via chmod/chown on /etc/passwd. (3) A crafted InitialPayload with a PortForwardSourceRequest could invoke PipeSocketHandler::listen() with an attacker-controlled SocketEndpoint — passing auth didn't prevent arbitrary file-permission changes.
techniques.impact: Direct lesson: (a) a passkey/pre-shared secret is necessary but not sufficient — the post-auth protocol must be hardened too; (b) any helper that spawns shells or manipulates filesystem paths must treat client-supplied data as untrusted even after authentication; (c) transport encryption and authentication are separate concerns — ET had one (SSH-bootstrapped passkey) but not the other (encrypted TCP). Do not replicate ET's unencrypted TCP relay.

techniques.name: mosh SSH-bootstrapped AES-128-OCB key model
techniques.how: mosh uses SSH as its bootstrap channel. mosh-client connects via SSH, which runs mosh-server on the host as the authenticated user. mosh-server generates a random 22-character base64 AES-128 session key and prints it to stdout over the SSH channel: 'MOSH CONNECT <UDP_PORT> <KEY>'. mosh-client receives this key, closes the SSH connection, and then uses UDP to the printed port. All subsequent UDP datagrams are encrypted with AES-128-OCB3 (authenticated encryption) keyed with that session key. The nonce is a 64-bit monotonic counter per direction. The security model: if an attacker cannot forge AES-OCB-authenticated packets they cannot hijack the session. The one-time key is never transmitted in cleartext — it travels over the SSH-encrypted channel. Weakness: if SSH is compromised or the user accepts an unknown SSH host key (TOFU leap of faith), the mosh key is also compromised.
techniques.impact: For the hybrid tool's terminal path, this pattern is directly applicable: use the pairing ceremony (SAS) and pre-shared identity keys for the initial connection instead of SSH, then derive a per-session symmetric key and encrypt the PTY byte stream with AES-GCM or ChaCha20-Poly1305. The improvement over mosh's model: no dependency on SSH; device keys are pre-pinned via the pairing ceremony so TOFU is eliminated.

techniques.name: Hardened Runtime + code signing for the PTY helper daemon
techniques.how: The macOS host-side helper that spawns PTY shells must be code-signed with the Hardened Runtime enabled (-o runtime in codesign). Without it, the binary is vulnerable to dylib injection, DYLD_INSERT_LIBRARIES attacks, and process memory tampering. Enable with: codesign -f -s <IDENTITY> -o runtime --entitlements helper-entitlements.plist helper-daemon. The Hardened Runtime by default enforces: (1) library validation (no unsigned dylibs can be injected), (2) no JIT, (3) no unsigned memory execution, (4) no DYLD variable injection. For a PTY-spawning helper, the minimal entitlements plist needs no extra keys — posix_openpt(), forkpty(), execve(shell) do not require special entitlements. The helper should NOT add com.apple.security.cs.allow-unsigned-executable-memory or com.apple.security.cs.disable-library-validation, which would undermine the protections. For notarization (optional but recommended for distribution), the binary must have Hardened Runtime enabled.
techniques.impact: Prevents local privilege escalation via dylib injection into the helper process. Does not prevent network-based attacks — that is the job of transport encryption and mutual auth. Does not sandbox the helper — it will still spawn a full user shell. Hardened Runtime alone is insufficient; it is one layer in a defense-in-depth stack. A compromised network attacker who gets past authentication still has full shell access.

techniques.name: NWListener interface scoping and rate limiting for connection attempts
techniques.how: NWListener does not natively support per-IP rate limiting. Implement in the newConnectionHandler: maintain a dictionary of [String: (count: Int, windowStart: Date)] keyed by remote IP. On each new connection, if count >= N within the window (e.g., 5 attempts in 60 seconds), call connection.cancel() immediately before any handshake and optionally add the IP to a temporary blocklist. For interface scoping: set parameters.requiredInterfaceType = .wifi (or .wiredEthernet) on the NWParameters to prevent the listener from accepting connections arriving on non-LAN interfaces (e.g., if a VPN tun interface is present). Note: requiredInterfaceType on NWListener has had bugs in some macOS versions where it affects advertising but not acceptance — verify on target OS. For a LaunchDaemon running as root, Local Network Privacy does not apply (macOS 15 confirmed: root-privilege code is exempt from the Local Network permission dialog, per Apple developer forums thread/763753).
techniques.impact: Reduces the brute-force surface for any password/passkey component. Interface scoping reduces accidental exposure if the machine is also on a VPN. The rate limiting must be applied before authentication processing completes — otherwise a flood of half-open connections can exhaust server-side state. SSH recommends 3-5 attempts per 60 seconds as a practical threshold.

techniques.name: Bonjour / mDNS discovery hardening
techniques.how: Using NWListener with a .service configuration (NWListener.Service(name:type:domain:)) advertises the service via mDNS, making it discoverable to any device on the LAN. This exposes: (1) the service type and port to passive scanners, (2) the hostname. Hardening options: (a) Do not advertise via Bonjour at all — require manual IP entry or a companion discovery protocol that only responds to devices presenting a valid HMAC token derived from a pre-shared pairing secret. (b) If Bonjour is used for convenience, advertise only the service type without sensitive metadata in TXT records. Apple's own documentation notes: 'Bonjour's design assumes cooperation of participants; in a hostile environment other mechanisms must be used.' mDNS has a known mDNSResponder privilege escalation CVE (2024) on both macOS and Windows via malicious multicast DNS queries.
techniques.impact: Bonjour advertisement is a reconnaissance shortcut for an attacker already on the LAN — they can find the service without port scanning. Disabling Bonjour advertisement and using a challenge-response discovery (client must prove knowledge of the pairing secret before the server acknowledges it) closes this information leak. This does not affect security of an established authenticated+encrypted session, but it narrows the attack surface.

techniques.name: Host-side authorization: run shell as logged-in user only, no privilege escalation path
techniques.how: The PTY helper should be configured in launchd (LaunchDaemon plist) with UserName set to the logged-in user, not root. If root is required for launch (e.g., to install the daemon), use a XPC service that drops privileges before spawning the PTY (setuid/setgid to the target user). The shell spawned via forkpty()/execve() must inherit only the necessary environment — strip DYLD_*, SUDO_*, and other injection vectors. Do not allow the client to specify the shell binary path or environment (analogous to the ET CVE where client-supplied SocketEndpoint paths were passed to privileged file operations). The shell should be fixed (e.g., /bin/zsh or the user's $SHELL from /etc/passwd), not client-configurable. Consider whether connecting remotely should require the screen to be unlocked (check CGSessionCopyCurrentDictionary() or loginwindow state before accepting connections).
techniques.impact: Limits blast radius: even if the authentication layer is somehow bypassed, the attacker gets a shell running as the logged-in user (not root). This is still dangerous but not immediately a system-wide compromise. The locked-screen check prevents shell access when the physical user is away from the machine. Avoids the ET pattern where a trusted-but-malicious client packet could affect /etc/passwd.

techniques.name: Keychain storage for long-term device identity keys (SecItemAdd / SecItemCopyMatching)
techniques.how: Store long-term static key material (your device's private key, and the dictionary of authorized peer public keys / device IDs) in the macOS/iOS Keychain, not in UserDefaults or a flat file. For private keys: SecItemAdd with kSecClass = kSecClassKey, kSecAttrAccessible = kSecAttrAccessibleWhenUnlockedThisDeviceOnly (most restrictive: never migrated to another device, not accessible when screen locked). For authorized peer public keys stored as generic passwords: kSecClass = kSecClassGenericPassword, kSecAttrAccessible = kSecAttrAccessibleAfterFirstUnlock (appropriate for a daemon that must authenticate after reboot without UI). Tag keys with kSecAttrApplicationTag for namespaced retrieval. On macOS, the Keychain is accessible to any process with the same team ID by default — if the daemon runs in a different process context, ensure access groups are configured correctly (kSecAttrAccessGroup).
techniques.impact: Prevents key material from being read by other apps (Keychain ACLs), from being included in iCloud backups (ThisDeviceOnly suffix), and from being migrated to a different machine. The kSecAttrAccessibleWhenUnlockedThisDeviceOnly choice means a screen-locked Mac cannot be attacked even if the attacker has physical access to the disk image — keys are only accessible while the user is logged in and screen is unlocked.

key_settings: NWProtocolTLS.Options + sec_protocol_options_set_local_identity: sets the local certificate/key identity for TLS handshake presentation (both host and client must set this)

sec_protocol_options_set_verify_block: custom trust evaluation callback — receives sec_trust_t, converts via sec_trust_copy_ref, compares cert DER hash against pinned known-devices list; call sec_protocol_verify_complete(true/false)
sec_protocol_options_set_peer_authentication_required(opts, true): on the NWListener's TLS options forces client to present a certificate (enables mutual TLS)
NWParameters.requiredInterfaceType = .wifi / .wiredEthernet: scopes NWListener to LAN interfaces only
kSecAttrAccessibleWhenUnlockedThisDeviceOnly: Keychain accessibility for private keys (most restrictive; not migratable, not accessible when locked)
kSecAttrAccessibleAfterFirstUnlock: for daemon-accessible peer key store (readable after first boot unlock, not when screen is just locked)
SecureEnclave.P256.KeyAgreement.PrivateKey(accessControl:authenticationContext:): hardware-backed key generation; dataRepresentation is an opaque blob for that SE only
LaunchDaemon plist UserName: run helper as the logged-in user, not root; prevents privilege escalation to root via shell
codesign -o runtime: enable Hardened Runtime to block dylib injection into the helper process
NWListener.newConnectionHandler: implement per-IP connection attempt rate limiting before any TLS handshake begins (5 attempts / 60 seconds threshold)
latency_numbers: TLS 1.3 full handshake: ~1 RTT (one round trip before application data) on LAN; typical completion <5ms on 100Mbit LAN
TLS 1.3 with session resumption (0-RTT): application data in first flight; reduces reconnect overhead to near-zero for a user who restores focus after screen dim
AES-GCM on Apple Silicon (ARM Cryptography Extensions): hardware-accelerated; measured ~10 GB/s throughput — negligible overhead on PTY byte stream (~10-100 KB/s peak)
Secure Enclave ECDH key agreement: measured ~5-10ms per operation on M1 (reported by community benchmarks); acceptable for once-per-session use, not per-packet
Noise_XX handshake: 3 messages (1.5 RTT); on LAN <10ms total including key derivation
SAS/pairing ceremony: one-time, human-paced — latency is irrelevant; target <5 seconds from scan to stored key
claims_to_verify: CLAIM: LaunchDaemon running as root is exempt from macOS Local Network Privacy permission dialog on macOS 15. Source: Apple developer forums thread/763753 (community forum, not official docs). Verify: test on macOS 15 with a non-sandboxed root LaunchDaemon using NWListener.

CLAIM: sec_protocol_options_set_peer_authentication_required(opts, true) on an NWListener forces the client to present a certificate and drops connections that do not. Verify: test with a plain NWConnection that has no local identity configured — confirm the server drops it before any application data is exchanged.

CLAIM: NWParameters.requiredInterfaceType = .wifi on an NWListener prevents acceptance of connections arriving on other interfaces (e.g., loopback, VPN tun). Several developer forum posts suggest this affects advertising but not acceptance in some macOS versions. Verify: test on macOS 14/15 with an active VPN.

CLAIM: SecureEnclave.P256.KeyAgreement.PrivateKey.dataRepresentation is device-specific and does not survive OS reinstall. Source: community blog and Apple forums. Verify: document the exact behavior (is it SE-instance-specific or just tied to the Keychain ACL?).
CLAIM: mosh uses AES-128-OCB3 (not OCB2). Source: hoop.dev security review blog. Verify against mosh source code (crypto/ocb.cc) or the mosh paper for the exact OCB version.

CLAIM: Eternal Terminal's TCP transport post-passkey-exchange has no encryption. Source: code analysis of SshSetupHandler.cpp and absence of TLS in TcpSocketHandler. Verify: check if any version of ET added TLS after the 2021 CVE disclosures.

CLAIM: Bonjour mDNSResponder has a 2024 privilege escalation CVE affecting macOS. Source: cybersecuritynews.com/bonjour-privilege-escalation. Verify CVE number and affected macOS versions against NVD.

CLAIM: WireGuard/Noise_IKpsk2 does not perform a separate pre-handshake key exchange — it assumes static public keys are already distributed out-of-band. Source: wireguard.com/protocol. This is load-bearing for the pairing model design.
open_questions: Which Noise pattern is optimal for this use case: Noise_XX (simpler, no prior state needed, 1.5 RTT) vs Noise_IK (requires server static key known at connect time — available after pairing, 1 RTT, zero-RTT first message)? The right answer depends on whether reconnection latency matters more than simplicity.

Should the Secure Enclave P256 identity key be used only for authentication (signing), with a separate Curve25519 ephemeral key for ECDH in each session? This is the standard split (SE for authentication, software key for forward-secret key agreement) — but the exact handshake binding needs design.

What is the UX surface for the pairing ceremony on iOS (client side)? The client must display or scan a code. Options: QR code camera scan of the host's displayed code, or Manual numeric entry. The QR path requires camera permission on iOS.

Should the host accept connections only when the macOS screen is unlocked and the user is logged in? This requires polling loginwindow state (CGSessionCopyCurrentDictionary or SCDynamicStore) — is this a desired security property or too restrictive for the daily-coding use case?

Is Tailscale-as-transport a viable alternative for this tool? Tailscale provides encryption + authentication for free, eliminates the need to implement any of the above, and works on LAN without its coordination server once keys are pre-distributed. Tradeoff: dependency on Tailscale daemon on both devices, privacy implications of Tailscale's coordination server seeing device metadata.

gapCorpus.dimension: File transfer design for a hybrid native-Swift remote coding tool (macOS host + macOS/iOS clients) with a custom Network.framework transport and no SSH/SFTP dependency
gapCorpus.summary: A custom LAN remote-coding tool needs an explicit file-transfer story because it owns the transport and SSH's free scp/sftp ride is absent. Five interlocking dimensions matter: (1) whether to multiplex a file channel over the existing connection or open a second NWConnection; (2) whether to piggyback zmodem-style over the PTY byte stream; (3) iOS sandbox + Files-app export/import; (4) macOS host file-access and entitlement model; (5) UX precedents from Blink, Termius, Secure Shellfish, and Jump Desktop. The recommended v1 is a multiplexed in-band channel using NWProtocolFramer (Apple-native), with chunked sends gated by NWConnection.SendCompletion back-pressure, per-chunk CRC32 integrity, and offset-based resume — surfaced on iOS via UIDocumentPickerViewController for export and a Share Extension for import, with no FileProvider complexity until v2.

techniques.name: In-band multiplexed file channel via NWProtocolFramer
techniques.how: Define a custom NWProtocolFramer that prepends a fixed header (1-byte channel-type: 0=PTY, 1=file-data, 2=file-control; 4-byte payload length; 1-byte flags) to every frame. Both PTY byte-stream frames and file-transfer frames share the single NWConnection already established for the session. On receive, the framer routes to the PTY renderer or the file-transfer state machine based on channel-type. NWProtocolFramer.Message carries per-frame metadata. Register the framer in NWParameters.defaultProtocolStack.applicationProtocols.
techniques.impact: Eliminates a second TCP handshake and a second authentication round-trip. Keeps NAT/firewall punch-through trivial (one port). Adds ~6 bytes per frame overhead. File bandwidth competes with PTY typing echo only under sustained large transfers — acceptable for LAN daily coding use.

techniques.name: Out-of-band second NWConnection for file transfers
techniques.how: When a file transfer is requested, open a new NWConnection to the host on a second port (or the same port with a distinguishing TLS ALPN token). Transfer runs independently; the PTY connection is undisturbed. Close the second connection when done.
techniques.impact: Simpler framing — raw bytes on the wire. But requires a second port, a second connection lifecycle, and a second auth/handshake per transfer session. On LAN this adds ~5-10ms setup cost. Complicates NAT and firewall configuration for end-users.

techniques.name: Chunked NWConnection sends with contentProcessed back-pressure
techniques.how: Call connection.send(content: chunk, contentContext: .defaultMessage, isComplete: false, completion: .contentProcessed { error in /* send next chunk */ }) and only issue the next chunk inside the contentProcessed closure. Chunk size 64 KB is conventional (matches TCP window and avoids in-memory queuing). Use DispatchIO.read(fromFileDescriptor:maxLength:runningHandlerOn:handler:) to stream file bytes off disk without loading the entire file into memory.
techniques.impact: Prevents the NWConnection internal send buffer from growing unboundedly (which it will do without gating — the connection happily accepts gigabytes). Keeps sender memory usage proportional to one or two chunks. DispatchIO on macOS host handles large build artifacts (100 MB+) safely.

techniques.name: Per-chunk CRC32 integrity + offset-based resume on reconnect
techniques.how: Prepend each file chunk with a 4-byte CRC32 checksum of the payload (compute with zlib's crc32 or Swift's Compression framework). The receiver verifies each chunk and sends an ACK with the next expected byte offset. On reconnect, the client sends a RESUME(fileID, offset) control message; the host seeks to that offset and resumes sending. Store pending transfer state (fileID, offset, total size, temp file path) in a lightweight in-memory dictionary keyed by connection session ID, persisted to UserDefaults/CoreData for cross-launch resume.
techniques.impact: On LAN, corruption is rare, but resume-on-reconnect is a genuine win: the app's own transport can drop and reconnect (Wi-Fi handoff, screen lock on iPad). Without resume, a 500 MB Xcode archive must restart from byte 0 after every reconnect. The tus protocol uses the same offset-HEAD pattern and validates it at scale.

techniques.name: Zmodem-over-PTY (rz/sz) triggered by escape sequence detection
techniques.how: The iTerm2 approach: watch the PTY output stream for the regex 'rz waiting to receive.**B0100' (triggers host-to-client send) and '**B00000000000000' (triggers client-to-host receive). On match, a silent coprocess (or, in this app's case, the app itself) intercepts the PTY byte stream and runs the ZMODEM protocol (lrzsz binaries on the host; a Swift receiver on the client). The escape sequences are part of the ZMODEM handshake. iTerm2 configures this as 'Run Silent Coprocess' triggers in Profiles > Advanced > Triggers.
techniques.impact: Zero-protocol-change: works through any existing SSH/PTY channel. But: (a) SwiftTerm has NO zmodem receiver — iTermContent delegate fires for OSC 1337 File= (inline=0 non-inline path) but that only hands raw base64 bytes to the host app, not a framed file download; (b) base64 encoding adds ~33% overhead; (c) transfers block the PTY — no concurrent input; (d) lrzsz must be installed on the host. For a bespoke tool this buys nothing over a dedicated channel and adds considerable complexity.

techniques.name: SwiftTerm iTermContent delegate hook (OSC 1337 File= non-inline)
techniques.how: SwiftTerm parses OSC 1337 sequences. When inline=0 (or absent), it does NOT render the data as an image; instead it calls the TerminalDelegate.iTermContent(source:content:) callback with the raw ArraySlice<UInt8> of the sequence payload. The host app or client app implements this delegate to extract the base64-encoded file bytes and filename from the key-value pairs (name=, size=, etc.) and write them to disk. To use this as a download path: the host emits ESC]1337;File=name=foo.tar.gz;size=12345;inline=0:<base64 data> BEL into the PTY stream; the client SwiftTerm view fires iTermContent and the app saves the file.
techniques.impact: Leverages an already-parsed OSC path in SwiftTerm with zero changes to the library. Suitable for small files (screenshots, log snippets <1 MB). Not suitable for large files: entire payload must be base64-encoded and embedded in a single OSC sequence, which SwiftTerm buffers in memory before firing the callback. No chunking, no back-pressure, no resume.

techniques.name: iOS export via UIDocumentPickerViewController
techniques.how: After a file is received into the app's sandbox (Documents/ or a temp URL), present UIDocumentPickerViewController(forExporting: [url], asCopy: true). The system Files picker lets the user choose a destination (Files app, iCloud Drive, USB drive, etc.). The copy lands outside the sandbox; your app retains its original. For Swift: present as a sheet from a SwiftUI View via .sheet or UIViewController. For multiple files, pass an array of URLs. The user sees standard Files UI.
techniques.impact: Zero custom UI. Works with all Files-compatible destinations. The 'asCopy: true' flag means the app's sandbox copy is preserved — correct for a remote file pull. No entitlement needed beyond the default sandbox.

techniques.name: iOS import via UIDocumentPickerViewController (open mode) or Share Extension
techniques.how: Two paths: (A) In-app open: UIDocumentPickerViewController(forOpeningContentTypes: [.data]) lets the user pick any file; delegate receives a security-scoped URL; call startAccessingSecurityScopedResource() before reading, stopAccessingSecurityScopedResource() when done, then copy bytes into the app sandbox before upload begins. (B) Share Extension: declare an NSExtension with NSExtensionActivationRule accepting kUTTypeData; user taps Share from Files/Photos and picks the app; the extension receives an NSItemProvider array; call loadFileRepresentation(forTypeIdentifier:) to get a temporary file URL, copy into the shared App Group container, post an intent to the main app via Darwin notifications or XPC.
techniques.impact: Path A is simpler for v1 and covers the primary use case (upload a file you navigated to in Files). Path B handles 'share from another app' workflows (e.g. share a screenshot from Photos directly to the remote host) but requires an extension target and App Group entitlement.

techniques.name: iOS Files.app FileProvider extension (NSFileProviderReplicatedExtension)
techniques.how: Implement NSFileProviderReplicatedExtension. The system calls fetchContents(for:version:request:) when the user taps a file in Files — the extension downloads from the host and returns a local URL. The system calls modifyItem(_:) / createItem(basedOn:) when the user edits/creates — the extension uploads to the host. Use PushKit PKPushType.fileProvider to receive push notifications of remote changes and trigger sync. Extension runs in its own process with a 20 MB memory limit; use streaming (DispatchIO/URLSession streaming) not whole-file loads. Available iOS 16+.
techniques.impact: This gives users a native Files.app location that looks like a local folder — the same UX as Secure Shellfish and Blink's replicated file provider. It is the highest-value UX but also the highest-complexity implementation. Recommended as v2 after the basic transfer channel works.

techniques.name: macOS host side: non-sandboxed helper with full home directory access
techniques.how: The host component is a non-sandboxed launchd agent or a privileged helper installed via SMJobBless/SMAppService. Without App Sandbox, it can read/write ~/Documents, ~/Desktop, arbitrary paths, and POSIX-permission-allowed locations. File enumeration uses FileManager.contentsOfDirectory or a recursive enumerator. DispatchIO.read reads file data efficiently. The host must still respect POSIX permissions (it runs as the user, not root) and TCC-protected locations (~/Desktop, ~/Documents, ~/Downloads require com.apple.security.files.user-selected.read-write or TCC approval even for non-sandboxed apps on macOS 10.15+).
techniques.impact: Simple: no security-scoped bookmark juggling for the common case (home directory files). The app must handle TCC prompts for protected folders. A one-time NSOpenPanel to pick a 'shared folder root' with a persisted security-scoped bookmark is the right pattern for sandboxed host apps.

techniques.name: UX pattern: persistent transfer queue with progress indicator (Termius / Blink model)
techniques.how: Maintain a transfer queue (array of TransferJob structs with fileID, direction, bytesTotal, bytesSent, status) as an ObservableObject. Surface it as a persistent sheet or sidebar drawer — not a blocking modal. Each row shows filename, direction arrow, progress bar, speed (bytes/s), and ETA. Allow pause/resume/cancel per job. On iOS, a badge count on the transfers tab icon shows active jobs. On reconnect, automatically resume incomplete jobs. Termius uses a dedicated 'Transfers' tab; Blink uses the replicated FileProvider's background sync.
techniques.impact: Users can initiate a large download (Xcode archive) and keep coding in the terminal without a blocking UI. Matching Termius/Blink UX reduces learning curve for users migrating from those tools.

key_settings: NWProtocolFramer frame header layout: 1-byte channel-type (0=PTY, 1=file-data, 2=file-control), 4-byte UInt32 big-endian payload length, 1-byte flags (0x01=last-chunk, 0x02=compressed)
NWConnection chunk size: 65536 bytes (64 KB) — matches common TCP window size; use .contentProcessed completion to gate next chunk
DispatchIO.stream on host side for reading large files off disk without loading into memory
CRC32 per chunk (4 bytes appended or prepended to each file-data frame payload); receiver ACKs with next expected offset
Transfer state persistence: struct { fileID: UUID, remotePath: String, localURL: URL, offset: Int64, totalSize: Int64 } stored in UserDefaults (small) or CoreData (large history)
iOS sandbox: received files land in FileManager.default.temporaryDirectory or app's Documents/; export via UIDocumentPickerViewController(forExporting:asCopy:true)
iOS import: UIDocumentPickerViewController(forOpeningContentTypes:[.data]) + startAccessingSecurityScopedResource() + copy to sandbox before upload

macOS host: non-sandboxed daemon has free POSIX access to home dir; sandboxed app needs com.apple.security.files.user-selected.read-write + NSOpenPanel + security-scoped bookmark for locations outside sandbox container
FileProvider extension memory limit: 20 MB on iOS — use streaming reads, never load whole file into memory
PushKit PKPushType.fileProvider for server-push-triggered sync in FileProvider extension (iOS 16+)
latency_numbers: NWConnection extra TCP handshake for out-of-band second connection: ~5-10 ms on LAN (claim_to_verify: measured on the specific LAN hardware)
NWConnection contentProcessed completion fires after data is handed to the kernel send buffer — not after the remote ACK; actual round-trip on LAN Gigabit is ~0.2-1 ms per chunk
ZMODEM over PTY: base64 encoding adds ~33% size overhead; binary ZMODEM framing adds ~10 bytes per 1024-byte sub-packet
Blink replicated FileProvider background sync poll: every 5 seconds for visited directories
FileProvider extension background execution budget: ~30 seconds per task on iOS before the system may suspend
iTerm2 zmodem trigger detection latency: synchronous regex scan on every incoming byte chunk — negligible for terminal speeds
claims_to_verify: SwiftTerm iTermContent: the non-inline (inline=0) OSC 1337 File= path calls iTermContent delegate and passes the full base64 payload — verified in SwiftTerm Terminal.swift:1953-1956 source. CLAIM: 'the entire payload must be buffered before the callback fires' — true by inspection (OSC string is accumulated in _osc before dispatchOsc is called) but worth confirming the maximum safe OSC buffer size in SwiftTerm's EscapeSequenceParser

NWConnection send buffer accepts 'gigabytes' without back-pressure — sourced from Apple Developer Forums thread/689059 but that thread is from 2021; behavior may have been adjusted in later OS versions
FileProvider extension 20 MB memory limit on iOS — sourced from Apple Developer Forums thread/739839 (upload in FileProvider); verify against current iOS 18 documentation
NSFileProviderReplicatedExtension requires iOS 16+ — confirm minimum deployment target in Apple's FileProvider docs
PushKit PKPushType.fileProvider availability — mentioned in WWDC Tech Talk 10067; confirm it ships in production iOS (not just developer preview)
Blink's 5-second pull interval — sourced from docs.blink.sh/advanced/files-app; may change across Blink versions
Jump Desktop Fluid protocol has no file transfer today and plans it in Fluid 2.0 — sourced from support forum post; verify current state
open_questions: Should the file-control sub-protocol support directory tree listing (ls-style enumeration) over the same channel, enabling a future file-browser pane, or is that out of scope for v1?

How should the host resolve a requested remote path that includes shell expansions (~/foo, $GOPATH/src) — expand via the PTY shell with `echo` before opening the file, or restrict to absolute paths only?

Does the app need bidirectional concurrent transfers (download a log while uploading a patch) on the same session? NWProtocolFramer multiplexing supports this, but the state machine complexity doubles.

For the FileProvider v2: the custom LAN transport is not a standard URLSession backend — NSFileProviderReplicatedExtension's networking is expected to use URLSession or similar. How to plumb a custom NWConnection inside the extension process without violating the 20 MB memory limit or background execution contract?

UIPasteboard file-URL items: can the client paste a file URL copied from Finder/Files on macOS into the iOS client and trigger an upload? The pasteboard dimension intersects with file transfer but the UTType/security-scope implications on iOS need validation.

For drag-and-drop on iPadOS: can the user drag a file from Files.app into the terminal/IDE window and trigger an upload? This requires UIDropInteraction + NSItemProvider + loadFileRepresentation — worth scoping for v1 if drag-drop is already in the GUI window path.

gapCorpus.summary: Three architecturally distinct approaches exist for N concurrent PTY shells in the hybrid coding tool: (1) app-level multiplexing of N channels over one Network.framework TCP connection using SSH-RFC-4254-style framing, (2) tmux control mode (-CC) where a single tmux client connection drives native Swift UI through a text-protocol, and (3) naive one-TCP-per-shell. For a v1 Swift-native LAN tool the recommended path is naive one-connection-per-shell plus an optional thin session-manager process on the host to keep PTYs alive across reconnects; tmux control mode is the recommended v2 upgrade path because it delivers server-side persistence for free and maps tmux windows/panes onto native UI at zero marginal cost, at the price of a protocol implementation layer (~2 000 lines as evidenced by iTerm2's TmuxGateway.m). App-level channel multiplexing is the correct v3/hardened option if the dependency on tmux is unacceptable and the team is prepared to implement flow-control framing and a custom reconnect/sequence-number layer.

techniques.name: Naive one-TCP-connection-per-PTY-session
techniques.how: Each shell tab opens an independent NWConnection (TCP) to the host. The host accepts, forks a PTY, and streams raw bytes bidirectionally. Session state is lost on disconnect unless a host-side daemon keeps the PTY alive (e.g., a lightweight process that inherits the PTY master fd and queues output). Blink Shell uses exactly this model: each swipeable tab is a distinct SSH or mosh connection with no multiplexing. SwiftTermApp (the open-core reference for SwiftTerm on iOS) likewise instantiates one SSHConnection / NIOSSHHandler / NIOSSHHandler.createChannel per session tab (sources/SwiftTerm/TerminalApp/iOSTerminal/UIKitSshTerminalView.swift lines 160-270). Reconnect means re-opening the connection and re-attaching to the persisted PTY, not replaying a byte stream.
techniques.impact: Lowest implementation complexity. Each NWConnection is independently flow-controlled by TCP. No head-of-line blocking between sessions. On LAN the per-connection overhead (TCP handshake ~0.5 ms, TLS ~1 ms) is negligible. Downside: no server-side persistence without a separate session-manager process; on disconnect the PTY dies unless explicitly protected.
sources: https://blink.sh/ (one mosh/SSH connection per tab, Mosh opens UDP ports 60000-60005 for up to 5 concurrent sessions)

techniques.name: App-level channel multiplexing (SSH RFC 4254 style) over one TCP connection
techniques.how: A single NWConnection carries N logical channels. Each channel has a locally unique integer ID. Wire framing follows RFC 4254: CHANNEL_OPEN {channel_type, sender_channel, initial_window_size, max_packet_size}, CHANNEL_DATA {recipient_channel, data}, CHANNEL_WINDOW_ADJUST {recipient_channel, bytes_to_add}, CHANNEL_CLOSE {recipient_channel}. Per-channel flow-control windows are independent: window_size starts at negotiated value (SSH default is 2^21 = 2 MB), and the sender MUST NOT exceed min(window_size, max_packet_size). The receiver issues WINDOW_ADJUST to re-open space. Channel IDs are endpoint-local: channel N on the client side maps to a different number on the server side; both sides maintain a lookup table. OpenSSH channels.c implements this exactly: channel_new() allocates a Channel with c->local_window = window and c->local_window_max = window; channel_register_fds() registers read/write fds per channel (channels.c lines 260-390 in openssh/openssh-portable channels.c). Head-of-line blocking: because all channels share one TCP byte stream, a large burst on channel 2 (e.g., a 'make' output flood) that fills the TCP send buffer will delay small keystrokes on channel 1 until TCP clears the backlog. Mitigation: set SO_PRIORITY or split into two TCP connections (interactive vs. bulk); on LAN with low RTT (~0.3 ms) and BDP of ~5 KB the window drains in under one frame interval even at 100 Mbit/s, so practical HOL impact is minimal for the stated use-case (30 fps, mostly static screens). RFC 4254 states: channel requests for distinct channels MAY be replied to out-of-order, so per-channel command round-trips do not block each other.
techniques.impact: One TCP handshake for N shells; saves N-1 connections. Requires implementing channel-open/close/data/window-adjust framing in Swift on both client and server. Back-pressure is per-channel, meaning a stalled channel does not slow others at the application layer, but all channels share the TCP send buffer (TCP-level HOL). The flow-control window must be sized correctly: too small and typing latency accumulates; too large and memory cost grows. 2 MB default from SSH is reasonable.
sources: https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc4254.html (sections 5.1-5.4: channel open, data, window adjust, close)

techniques.name: tmux control mode (-CC) with native Swift UI mapping
techniques.how:
The client connects one TCP/SSH connection and launches 'tmux -CC new-session -A'. tmux enters control mode: it sends a DCS sequence \033P1000p on entry, then streams line-delimited text notifications on stdout and accepts tmux commands on stdin. HANDSHAKE: client receives %session-changed $<id> <name> (and withholds writes until this arrives, as seen in iTerm2's TmuxGateway.m parseSessionChangeCommand / enableWrites). PANE OUTPUT: every byte the shell writes appears as '%output %<pane_id> <octal-escaped-data>
'. EXTENDED OUTPUT (tmux >= 3.2 with pause mode): '%extended-output %<pane_id> <latency_ms> [extra_args] : <data>'. ALL tmux windows/sessions map as: %window-add @<wid>, %window-close @<wid>, %layout-change @<wid> <layout-string>, %session-changed, %sessions-changed, %window-renamed, %window-pane-changed, %pane-mode-changed, %pause/%continue for flow control. COMMANDS: client writes 'new-window', 'split-window -h', 'send-keys -t %<pane> <keys> Enter', etc. RESPONSES are wrapped: '%begin <timestamp> <cmd_number> <flags>
...response lines...
%end <timestamp> <cmd_number> <flags>
'. Command number and flags must match. Flags bit 1: client-originated command (=1); bit 0: server-originated (=0). FLOW CONTROL: 'refresh-client -f pause-after=N' enables it; tmux sends %pause %<pane> when client falls behind, client resumes with 'refresh-client -A '%<pane>:continue''. iTerm2 implements this in full in TmuxGateway.m (884 lines) and TmuxController.m (1100+ lines), mapping each tmux window to a native PTYTab/PTYSession. iTerm2 uses an NSMutableDictionary windowPanes_ keyed by pane ID to route %output to the correct TerminalView. Parse loop: token per line, dispatch on prefix. SwiftTerm has NO built-in tmux control mode parser; a custom layer (a Swift TmuxGateway equivalent) is needed.
techniques.impact: Server-side session persistence for free: all PTYs live inside tmux, survive disconnect, reconnect is 'tmux -CC attach'. All N shells are carried over ONE TCP/SSH connection with no extra framing beyond the tmux control protocol. The client gets native UI tabs/panes driven by %window-add and %layout-change notifications. Cost: tmux must be installed on host (standard on macOS via Homebrew/Xcode CLI tools, or pre-installed on most Linux servers). Protocol parsing layer of ~1500-2500 lines (based on iTerm2 source size). The command queue is single-threaded (one outstanding begin/end block at a time), which serializes tmux commands but does not affect pane output delivery (notifications are interleaved freely between command blocks). Not compatible with mosh (mosh does not carry the tmux control stream faithfully). Eternal Terminal explicitly supports this: 'tmux control center is supported in ET since it does not rely on the ssh protocol.'
sources: https://github.com/tmux/tmux/wiki/Control-Mode (full protocol spec: notifications, command format, flow control, subscriptions)
https://github.com/gnachman/iTerm2 (sources/tmux/TmuxGateway.m lines 1-884: full parser; TmuxController.m: window/pane lifecycle)
https://eternalterminal.dev/howitworks/ (ET supports tmux -CC because it is its own protocol over a reliable stream)

techniques.name: Host-side session manager (PTY keeper daemon)
techniques.how: A lightweight host-side process (written in Swift or a shell script wrapping script(1) / dtach / GNU screen) adopts the PTY master fd when the client disconnects. On reconnect the client re-attaches by opening a new socket to the daemon, which replays buffered output and resumes forwarding. This is architecturally what Eternal Terminal's BackedWriter/BackedReader does: BackedWriter keeps an encrypted ring buffer of the last N bytes; on reconnect the client sends its sequence number and BackedWriter replays the delta. This approach is independent of tmux. The session manager runs as a per-user launchd agent on macOS (native) or a systemd user unit. For a Swift app targeting a known Mac host, this can be a small Swift actor that holds a [UUID: PTYSession] dictionary.
techniques.impact: Adds server-side persistence without requiring tmux. Complexity is lower than full tmux control mode but higher than naive one-connection-per-shell. Does not provide split-pane or window management on its own. Composes cleanly with both approach 1 and approach 2.

techniques.name: QUIC streams via Network.framework NWMultiplexedConnection
techniques.how: Apple's Network.framework supports QUIC natively. An NWConnection with a QUIC NWProtocolQUIC parameters set can open multiple independent QUIC streams. Each stream is independent at the transport layer: a lost packet in stream 2 does NOT block stream 1 (no TCP-level HOL blocking). This eliminates the key downside of SSH-style channel multiplexing over TCP. API: NWConnection with .quic options, then use NWConnection.send/receive per stream. Requires iOS 15+/macOS 12+. Host-side would require a QUIC server (e.g., a Swift actor using Network.framework listener with .quic). No standard 'terminal over QUIC' protocol exists yet; the framing and session protocol would be entirely custom.
techniques.impact: Eliminates TCP-level HOL blocking between sessions. Requires iOS 15+/macOS 12+. Requires building a custom server on the host (no off-the-shelf option). Significantly increases implementation scope for v1. Primarily relevant if sessions share a constrained network link and burst traffic causes measurable latency (unlikely on LAN). Suitable as a v3/future option.

key_settings: tmux control mode wire format: each line is one notification or one command-response fragment, terminated by \n. On entry with -CC tmux sends DSC \033P1000p. Client must withhold writes until %session-changed is received (iTerm2 TmuxGateway.m: enableWrites is called from parseSessionChangeCommand).

tmux %output octal escaping: all bytes < ASCII 32 and backslash are replaced with three-digit octal (e.g., newline = \012, ESC = \033, backslash = \134). The decoder in iTerm2's TmuxGateway.m decodeEscapedOutput: reads exactly three octal digits after each backslash.

%extended-output format (tmux >= 3.2): '%extended-output %<pane_id> <latency_microseconds> [extra args] : <encoded_data>'. The colon-space separator marks the start of payload. Latency is integer microseconds.

%begin/%end flags: bit 0 = server-originated response (flags & 1 == 0), bit 1 = client-originated (flags & 1 == 1). If flags & 1 == 0, iTerm2 treats it as an auto-response (server side effect) and does not pop from the command queue.

iTerm2 command queue is a strict FIFO (NSMutableArray commandQueue_). Only one %begin/%end block is in flight at a time. Commands for unrelated panes do NOT block %output delivery; notifications are delivered freely between command blocks.

RFC 4254 channel initial window size default: OpenSSH uses CHAN_SES_WINDOW_DEFAULT = 2^21 bytes (2 MB) and CHAN_SES_PACKET_DEFAULT = 32768 bytes (32 KB max packet). Both are negotiated per channel at open time.

RFC 4254 flow control: sender MUST NOT send more than min(window_size, max_packet_size) bytes per write. Receiver issues SSH_MSG_CHANNEL_WINDOW_ADJUST to open more space. Per-channel, independent of other channels.

Eternal Terminal session identity: after initial SSH bootstrap, ET generates a session ID and password, starts an ETServer process, and closes SSH. The client reconnects directly to ETServer using the session ID + password. Session-level reconnect, not connection-level replay.

SwiftTerm LocalProcessTerminalView: connects a TerminalView to a Unix PTY running a local process. SshTerminalView (SwiftTerm TerminalApp) uses one SSHConnection (NIOSSHHandler + one session channel) per view instance. There is no built-in session manager or multi-shell multiplexer.

SwiftTerm Thread safety: as of recent releases, CharData tables are owned per Terminal instance (not static), making parallel Terminal instances safe. Each session's TerminalView can run independently.

Blink Shell session model: each tab = independent mosh/SSH connection. mosh uses UDP ports 60000-60005 (space for 5 concurrent sessions). No app-level multiplexing. Blink relies on mosh's SSP reconnect for transport resilience, not session replay.
latency_numbers: TCP handshake on LAN (Gigabit Ethernet, ~0.3 ms RTT): approximately 0.3-0.5 ms per new NWConnection — negligible for session setup, irrelevant for steady-state typing.

SSH channel multiplexing HOL worst case on LAN: BDP = RTT × bandwidth = 0.3 ms × 125 MB/s ≈ 37.5 KB. A 37.5 KB burst on one channel could delay a 1-byte keystroke on another channel by up to one RTT (0.3 ms) until TCP clears the backlog. At 30 fps this is sub-frame and below the stated non-goal of <16 ms absolute latency.

tmux control mode command latency: each tmux command (e.g., 'new-window') incurs one round-trip to the host plus tmux server processing. On LAN this is ~0.5-1 ms. iTerm2 batches commands using sendCommandList: (a NSArray of dictionaryForCommand: entries sent as a single write) to amortize RTT cost.

tmux %output delivery latency: zero extra hops — tmux writes the notification to the control client immediately when the pty produces output. The octal-encode pass is in-process in the tmux server, adding microseconds.

Eternal Terminal reconnect time: ET's BackedWriter/BackedReader replays only the delta (bytes since client's last ack'd sequence number). On LAN with a short disconnect this is milliseconds; the session resumes mid-line without reattach delay.
claims_to_verify: RFC 4254 out-of-order channel replies: the spec states 'channel requests for distinct channels MAY be replied to out-of-order' (section 5.4). Verify that a Swift custom implementation honours this to avoid unnecessary command serialisation.

tmux control mode: the claim that %output notifications are never interleaved inside a %begin/%end block is stated in the tmux wiki ('A notification will never occur inside an output block'). Verify against the actual tmux server source (control.c / cmd-control-client.c in nicm/tmux, which was not indexable by Sourcegraph during this session).

SwiftTerm parallel Terminal instances: recent release notes say CharData tables are now per-Terminal (not static) for thread safety. Verify that the Terminal VT100 parser itself is re-entrant when driven from concurrent DispatchQueues (one per session).

Eternal Terminal no SSH protocol: ET docs say it 'does not implement any of the SSH protocol.' Verify whether ET's initial bootstrap still requires an SSH connection to establish the session password, or whether this changed in recent versions.

mosh incompatibility with tmux control mode: the claim that mosh does not faithfully carry the tmux DCS/escape-sequence control stream is widely stated but should be tested; mosh's SSP layer does transmit arbitrary terminal escape sequences — the issue may be with mosh's local echo prediction modifying the control-mode byte stream rather than a fundamental incompatibility.

NWMultiplexedConnection for QUIC streams: Apple's documentation mentions NWConnection can carry QUIC streams; verify the exact API for opening additional streams on an existing QUIC connection (NWConnection vs. NWProtocolQUIC API in iOS 15+ / macOS 12+ SDK).
open_questions: Should the app own the tmux session (launch 'tmux new-session' on connect) or attach to an existing user tmux (attach-session -A)? The -A flag (create if none, attach if exists) is the iTerm2 recommended pattern and is architecturally the right choice for session persistence, but requires a decision about session naming/identity per-client.

How should the client handle the case where tmux is not installed on the host? This is the main portability risk of approach 2. Options: bundle a static tmux binary in the host helper app, require it as a prerequisite, or fall back to approach 1.

For v1 one-connection-per-shell: should the host helper process keep PTYs alive on disconnect (using a detach/re-attach model) or let them die? The answer determines whether 'resume previous session' is a feature at launch. Keeping PTYs alive requires a launchd agent or equivalent on the host.

tmux window vs. pane granularity: iTerm2 maps tmux *windows* to native tabs and tmux *panes* to native splits. Should the hybrid tool follow the same mapping (where a tab = tmux window and a split = tmux pane) or flatten everything to tabs? The iTerm2 constraint is that 'a tab with a tmux window may not contain non-tmux split panes' — mixing tmux and non-tmux sessions in one tab is not supported.

Is the command serialisation in tmux control mode (one %begin/%end in-flight at a time, strict FIFO queue) a problem at scale? At N=10 sessions each firing a command simultaneously, the commands queue up and the last one waits for N RTTs. For a daily coding use-case this is not a real-time constraint, but worth acknowledging as a latency budget item.

Does the transport for approach 2 (tmux -CC) need to be inside an SSH tunnel for security on LAN, or is an application-level TLS connection over Network.framework sufficient? This affects the session setup complexity and whether tmux's DCS-based entry works transparently over the custom transport.

gapCorpus.dimension: Session persistence, reconnection correctness, and roaming for the TCP-based terminal path
gapCorpus.summary: The hybrid app's TCP terminal path faces four concrete failure modes: (1) replay of a raw-VT-byte ring buffer that was truncated mid-escape-sequence corrupts the emulator; (2) iOS/iPadOS backgrounds aggressively (~30 s budget) and kills the underlying socket; (3) macOS lid-close forces system sleep regardless of IOPMAssertion type, dropping all TCP connections; (4) Wi-Fi↔Ethernet roaming silently invalidates the TCP socket. Eternal Terminal's BackedReader/BackedWriter pattern is directly applicable: sequence-numbered packet-level buffering with an ACK-based catchup exchange on reconnect, operating at the packet boundary (not raw byte boundary), which eliminates the mid-escape-sequence truncation hazard. Mosh sidesteps this entirely via state-snapshot SSP but requires UDP and the project has rejected that path. The correct design for TCP is ET-style packet framing + a DECSTR/RIS prefix before replaying only the safe ring-buffer tail, plus tmux or a persistent helper process holding the master PTY fd to survive any client disconnect duration. iOS requires an explicit architecture decision: reconnect eagerly on foreground return (scenePhase .active) rather than trying to keep the socket alive across suspension. macOS wake is reliably detectable via NSWorkspaceDidWakeNotification.

techniques.name: ET-style packet-framed ring buffer with sequence-numbered ACK catchup
techniques.how: Eternal Terminal's BackedWriter buffers up to 64 MB of encrypted, length-prefixed packets in a deque, each tagged with an incrementing sequenceNumber. On reconnect, the client sends its reader sequenceNumber (last successfully received packet index) in a SequenceHeader protobuf; the server calls writer->recover(lastValidSequenceNumber) to compute how many packets to re-transmit, packs them into a CatchupBuffer protobuf, and both sides call BackedReader::revive(newFd, recoveredMessages) and BackedWriter::revive(newFd) to resume. Because the unit is a complete encrypted packet (not a raw byte slice), replay always starts at a packet boundary — the mid-escape-sequence truncation hazard is structurally eliminated. The BackedWriter::MAX_BACKUP_BYTES cap is 64 MB; if the buffer is evicted, recover() throws 'Client is too far behind server' and the session must be abandoned.
techniques.impact: Eliminates replay-corruption risk for the terminal text path; gives the client up to 64 MB of session history across any-duration disconnects. Adds ~one RTT of reconnect overhead for the sequence exchange. Requires the app to frame its PTY byte stream into discrete packets rather than streaming raw bytes.

techniques.name: DECSTR prefix before replay of ring-buffer tail (raw-byte path fallback)
techniques.how: If the app does NOT adopt packet framing and must replay raw VT bytes from a ring buffer, send ESC [ ! p (DECSTR, Soft Terminal Reset) to the SwiftTerm instance before replaying the buffer tail. DECSTR resets cursor visibility, insert mode, origin mode, autowrap, character sets (G0-G3), SGR attributes, cursor position (home), scroll margins, and keyboard modes — the modal state that causes corruption when replayed mid-sequence. What DECSTR does NOT reset: screen cell content, scrollback buffer, and alternate screen state. SwiftTerm exposes this as Terminal.softReset() / Terminal.cmdSoftReset() (Sources/SwiftTerm/Terminal.swift:3480-3517). A full reset via Terminal.resetToInitialState() (Terminal.swift:5179) additionally clears all buffer contents and Kitty images — appropriate for a clean reconnect but wipes scrollback. The truncation hazard remains unless the ring buffer always starts at a safely parseable boundary (e.g., immediately after a complete escape sequence). Alternatively, the host can emit a known sync-point marker (e.g., a no-op DCS sequence) periodically; the client scans the replay buffer for the last such marker and discards everything before it.
techniques.impact: Reduces visible corruption on reconnect without requiring packet framing. Does not fully eliminate the mid-sequence hazard — a multi-byte escape sequence straddling the ring-buffer wrap point can still misbehave after reset. Combined with a sync-point scanner this becomes robust.

techniques.name: Mosh SSP: current-state snapshot, not byte-stream replay
techniques.how: Mosh's State Synchronization Protocol (SSP) never replays a byte stream. The server maintains a Terminal::Complete object (the authoritative screen framebuffer) and sends compact diffs between the last-acknowledged remote state and the current state over UDP. The client applies the diff to its local framebuffer. On 'reconnect' (client IP change or reappearance after loss), the server simply receives a UDP packet from the new source address with a sequence number greater than any prior one and updates its target; no replay occurs, no byte-stream boundary problem exists. Roaming is handled in network.cc::recv_one(): if server && remote_addr != packet_remote_addr, the server silently adopts the new client address. The mosh-server process ignores SIGHUP (sigaction SIG_IGN at startup) and runs until an explicit timeout (MOSH_SERVER_NETWORK_TMOUT, default 0 = no timeout).
techniques.impact: Architecturally immune to replay corruption and mid-sequence hazards. Roaming is automatic. Requires UDP, which the project has rejected for the terminal path. Included here as the canonical reference for why state-sync beats byte-stream replay.

techniques.name: Persistent PTY via helper process holding master fd (tmux-style isolation)
techniques.how: The host spawns a lightweight server/helper process that calls forkpty() and holds the master fd. Because the helper owns the master fd — not the per-client TCP connection handler — closing any client socket does not cause the kernel to deliver SIGHUP to the shell's process group. In ET's TerminalHandler the childPid/masterFd are owned by a long-lived TerminalHandler object in the server process, entirely decoupled from any BackedReader/BackedWriter; stop() calls kill(childPid, SIGKILL) only when explicitly requested. tmux's architecture is the extreme version: a background server process holds all master fds and sessions survive indefinitely until the shell exits or tmux is killed. For the hybrid app, the host binary should be a persistent daemon (launchd plist with KeepAlive=true on macOS, or a simple loop) that retains all master fds. Clients connect/disconnect freely. PTY lifetimes should be configurable (e.g., idle timeout after N minutes of no client) to avoid accumulating abandoned shells.
techniques.impact: Shell process and PTY survive client disconnect of any duration: lid-close, iPad sleep, Wi-Fi handoff, explicit app termination. This is the correct architectural answer to 'how long does the PTY live' — the answer is 'until the server decides to kill it', decoupled from client connectivity.

techniques.name: iOS client: eager reconnect on foreground (scenePhase .active) rather than background keep-alive
techniques.how: iOS gives a foregrounding app ~30 seconds of background execution time via beginBackgroundTask(withName:expirationHandler:) — confirmed by Apple documentation and community sources. The NWConnection underlying socket is reclaimed by the OS when the app is suspended (iOS reclaims sockets per TN2277). There is no supported API to keep a plain TCP NWConnection alive across suspension without a special entitlement. The correct design: (1) when the app transitions to background (UIScene.ActivationState / scenePhase .background), call connection.cancel() immediately and mark the session as disconnected; (2) when returning to foreground (scenePhase .active), immediately create a new NWConnection and perform the ET-style sequence-number exchange to resume; (3) NWConnection in .waiting(NWError.posixError(.ENETDOWN)) state with .waitingForConnectivity set will automatically advance to .ready when the path becomes satisfied — this handles brief network-gap transitions (e.g., walking through a dead zone) without an app lifecycle event. For macOS client: NWPathMonitor.pathUpdateHandler fires on Wi-Fi↔Ethernet switch; on path change, cancel the stale connection and create a new one; the sequence exchange picks up where the server left off. The NWConnection.restart() method (not forceRestart; the method is called restart() on NWConnection in recent SDKs) can re-attempt a waiting connection without a path change.
techniques.impact: Eliminates the illusion that a TCP socket survives iOS suspension. Forces the architecture to treat reconnect as a normal fast path rather than an exceptional recovery. Combined with ET-style buffering on the host, the user sees output resume seamlessly within one RTT of the app returning to foreground.

techniques.name: macOS host: NSWorkspaceDidWakeNotification for post-sleep listener recovery
techniques.how: macOS lid-close forces system sleep regardless of IOPMAssertion type — kIOPMAssertionTypePreventSystemSleep and kIOPMAssertionTypeNoIdleSleep are both overridden by lid-close per Apple's IOPMLib documentation. The host helper process cannot prevent the Mac from sleeping. Instead, subscribe to NSWorkspaceDidWakeNotification (NSWorkspace notification center, not defaultCenter) to receive the wake event. In the handler, re-listen on the NWListener and check NWPathMonitor for path availability before accepting connections. NWConnections that were .ready at sleep time will have transitioned to .failed or .cancelled by wake; tear them down and let clients reconnect. The host helper should be registered as a launchd KeepAlive agent so it is restarted if killed during sleep. For App Nap prevention during active sessions, hold an NSProcessInfo activity token with NSActivityUserInitiated (prevents App Nap AND system idle sleep; use NSActivityUserInitiatedAllowingIdleSystemSleep to allow idle sleep but prevent App Nap). Note: NSActivityUserInitiated does NOT prevent lid-close sleep.
techniques.impact: Ensures the host re-establishes its NWListener within seconds of wake, so the client's reconnect attempt (triggered by NWPathMonitor.pathUpdateHandler on the Mac client, or by scenePhase .active on iOS) finds an active server. Without this, clients reconnect but find no listener until the helper process naturally polls.

techniques.name: macOS client Wi-Fi↔Ethernet roaming: NWPathMonitor + connection recreation
techniques.how: When a MacBook client docks (Wi-Fi → Ethernet) or undocks (Ethernet → Wi-Fi), the TCP socket is silently invalidated. NWPathMonitor.pathUpdateHandler fires with a new NWPath; observe path.status == .satisfied && path != lastPath to trigger reconnect. NWConnection.viabilityUpdateHandler fires false when the path becomes non-viable, which is the signal to cancel and recreate. The recommended pattern: cancel the stale NWConnection on viabilityUpdateHandler(false), then in pathUpdateHandler when status == .satisfied create a new NWConnection to the same host:port and perform the sequence-number handshake. Because the server's BackedWriter buffer persists across this gap (it is in-process in the helper, not tied to the socket), the catchup exchange delivers any buffered output since the last ACK. On iPadOS, the same pattern applies across Wi-Fi network changes (e.g., home Wi-Fi → cellular, or different AP).
techniques.impact: Makes roaming transparent to the user: output that arrived while the path was broken is delivered immediately after the 1-RTT sequence exchange completes. The reconnect path is the same code as the iPad-foreground path, reducing special-case branching.

key_settings: ET BackedWriter::MAX_BACKUP_BYTES = 64 * 1024 * 1024 (64 MB) — packets evicted FIFO when connected; never evicted when disconnected (BackedWriter.hpp line 24-25)
ET BackedWriter::DISCONNECT_BUFFER_BYTES = 4 * 1024 * 1024 (4 MB) — write() returns SKIPPED rather than blocking when disconnected and buffer exceeds this limit (BackedWriter.hpp line 26-27)
iOS beginBackgroundTask background time: ~30 seconds hard limit regardless of task count (Apple Developer Forums thread/695910, confirmed by multiple community sources)
mosh-server MOSH_SERVER_NETWORK_TMOUT: default 0 (no timeout); set via env var; client-side default is 60 s before showing 'warning' overlay (mosh-server.cc)
NSActivityUserInitiated: prevents App Nap AND system idle sleep; does NOT prevent lid-close sleep (Apple power efficiency guide)
NSActivityUserInitiatedAllowingIdleSystemSleep: prevents App Nap only; allows system idle sleep (Apple power efficiency guide)
kIOPMAssertionTypePreventSystemSleep / kIOPMAssertionTypeNoIdleSleep: both overridden by lid-close, user-initiated sleep, thermal emergency (IOPMLib docs)
SwiftTerm Terminal.softReset() implements DECSTR: resets modes/attributes/cursor-home; does NOT clear screen content or scrollback (Terminal.swift:3480-3517)
SwiftTerm Terminal.resetToInitialState() implements RIS: full reset including buffer contents and Kitty images (Terminal.swift:5179-5190)

ET Connection::recover() sequence: lock both mutexes → write local SequenceHeader → read remote SequenceHeader → write CatchupBuffer → read remote CatchupBuffer → revive reader and writer (Connection.cpp:96-141)
NWConnection .waitingForConnectivity: connection waits for path to become satisfied; automatically advances to .ready — no explicit reconnect needed for brief gaps
latency_numbers: ET reconnect overhead: 1 full RTT for SequenceHeader + CatchupBuffer exchange (2 back-and-forth protobufs per side), plus TLS/crypto re-handshake overhead
iOS background execution window: ~30 seconds from beginBackgroundTask; socket reclaimed by OS on suspension (not just background)
Mosh idle keepalive interval: ~200 ms (nanosleep in the serve() spin loop)
ET reconnect retry loop: sleeps 1 second between attempts (ClientConnection::pollReconnect, Thread.sleep_for(seconds(1)))
SwiftTerm screen refresh after resetToInitialState: synchronous, bounded by rows×cols cell invalidation — negligible for typical 80×24 to 220×50 terminal sizes
claims_to_verify: iOS background time limit is ~30 seconds: Apple docs say 'approximately 30 seconds' but the actual enforcement is watchdog-based and can vary; measure in your specific app with UIApplication.shared.backgroundTimeRemaining

NWConnection socket is reclaimed on app suspension: sourced from TN2277 (archived) and community forums — verify with current Apple DTS; behavior may differ for apps with certain entitlements (VoIP, NEAppProxyProvider)

kIOPMAssertionTypePreventSystemSleep is overridden by lid-close: stated clearly in IOPMLib docs but the exact behavior with clamshell mode (closed lid + external display) is distinct — lid-close with external display connected may NOT sleep

ET BackedWriter buffer is never evicted when disconnected: confirmed by reading the source (BackedWriter.cpp: while (socketFd >= 0 && backupSize > MAX_BACKUP_BYTES)) but the DISCONNECT_BUFFER_BYTES=4MB SKIPPED return means new writes are dropped if buffer exceeds 4 MB while disconnected — this is a data-loss boundary that needs explicit handling in the app

NSWorkspaceDidWakeNotification is delivered to launchd agents (not just GUI apps): launchd KeepAlive agents are background processes; NSWorkspace notifications require a running run loop — verify that a non-GUI Swift daemon receives this notification reliably
NWConnection.restart() vs forceRestart() naming: Apple renamed/restructured this method across SDK versions; verify the exact method signature in the target deployment SDK

DECSTR cursor-home side effect: vt100.net docs confirm DECSTR moves cursor to home; this means any replay of buffered output after DECSTR starts at row 0 col 0, which is correct only if the replay delivers a complete terminal 'frame' — verify against SwiftTerm behavior with a test case
open_questions: Packet framing granularity: should each PTY read() call (up to 16 KB in ET's BUF_SIZE) be one packet, or should the app do line-at-a-time framing? Larger packets amortize sequence-number overhead but increase the minimum replay unit; smaller packets waste CPU on per-packet crypto.

What is the right session lifetime policy for orphaned PTYs on the host? ET's TerminalHandler only terminates on explicit stop() or child process exit — there is no idle timeout in the default configuration. For a coding tool, a configurable idle-kill timer (e.g., 48 hours) seems appropriate.

iPad cellular handoff: when iPadOS switches from Wi-Fi to cellular (or vice versa) while the app is foregrounded, does scenePhase change at all, or does only NWConnection.viabilityUpdateHandler fire? The reconnect trigger needs to be wired to the right event.

launchd KeepAlive + NSWorkspace: can a Swift command-line daemon registered as a launchd agent reliably receive NSWorkspaceDidWakeNotification? This requires a running CFRunLoop/NSRunLoop and registration with NSWorkspace, which is a Cocoa-layer API — needs empirical verification on macOS 14/15.

Should the host emit a periodic 'sync-point' no-op sequence into the PTY output stream so that a raw-byte ring buffer (if ever adopted) can be scanned for a safe replay starting point? What is a safe, universally parseable no-op in the VT stream that SwiftTerm will ignore but that serves as a known parser-reset boundary?

Buffer overflow decision: when the host BackedWriter exceeds DISCONNECT_BUFFER_BYTES (4 MB) while a client is disconnected, new PTY output is silently dropped. For a coding session running a long build, this means build output is lost. Is 4 MB sufficient, or should the disconnect buffer be larger (or unlimited, bounded by RAM)?
